visits.text: Fall in love with Aberaeron, a gorgeous Georgian coastal town on Cardigan Bay that ranks among Wales' most photogenic destinations. The colorful townhouses lining the harbor in shades of pink, blue, yellow and green create a rainbow of facades reflected in the calm waters below. This planned Regency town, built in the early 19th century, retains its elegant proportions and charming character.

Stroll along Aberaeron's picturesque harbor, where traditional fishing boats bob alongside pleasure craft against a backdrop of painted Georgian houses. The harbor promenade offers perfect vantage points for photography, especially during the magical golden hour when sunset light illuminates the colorful facades. Local seafood restaurants serve the day's catch in this authentic Welsh fishing community.

Discover New Quay, a seaside town just 20 minutes south along Cardigan Bay famous for its layered colorful houses cascading down to sandy beaches. This charming village, said to have inspired Dylan Thomas's Under Milk Wood, offers spectacular coastal scenery and excellent opportunities for dolphin watching in the bay. The tiered rows of brightly painted cottages create stunning compositions against the blue waters of the Irish Sea.

activities.text: Watch for bottlenose dolphins in Cardigan Bay, home to the largest resident population in the UK

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia

visits.text: Ride the historic Aberystwyth Cliff Railway, a charming funicular that has carried passengers up Constitution Hill since 1896. This Victorian engineering marvel offers one of the most spectacular viewpoints in Wales, revealing panoramic vistas over the town, Cardigan Bay and distant mountain peaks on clear days. The gentle ascent provides time to appreciate the beautifully maintained original carriages and mechanisms.

Explore Aberystwyth, a vibrant university town and seaside resort that serves as the cultural capital of Welsh-speaking Wales. The sweeping Victorian promenade curves around Cardigan Bay, lined with colorful hotels and guesthouses that have welcomed visitors since the railway age. The ruins of Aberystwyth Castle, perched on a headland, add historic drama to the coastal views.

Visit the National Library of Wales, one of only six copyright libraries in the UK, housing a vast collection of Welsh literature, manuscripts and historical records. The impressive Edwardian building on Penglais Hill overlooks the town and contains treasures including medieval Welsh poetry and the earliest surviving manuscript of the Mabinogion. The library's exhibitions bring Welsh culture and history vividly to life.

activities.text: Walk along the Victorian promenade and watch the sunset over Cardigan Bay from the seafront

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia
Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

visits.text: Look for the notice at the County Hall entrance: 'Of all market buildings with an open ground floor, this is the largest in England,' Pevsner said. Built between 1678 and 1682 by Christopher Kempster—the man who carved the stone for St Paul's Cathedral with Wren. Baroque in Oxfordshire limestone, giant Corinthian pilasters. Museum free, roof access £2.

activities.text: Sweet buns have been falling from the sky since 1761: at every major royal occasion, the mayor and councillors in ceremonial robes climb onto the County Hall roof and throw hundreds of currant buns to the crowd below. The tradition has been documented since the coronation of George III. No event scheduled? Look for bun-throwing newspapers in the ground-floor museum.

days_plan.itin_title: Thames & Vale of White Horse: 6 days Loop

places.name: Aldeburgh

visits.text: Southwold is Suffolk's most elegant seaside resort: colourful houses, a shingle beach with rows of white and blue painted bathing huts, an immaculate white lighthouse dominating the town since 1890. Don't miss the Pier, built in 1900 and fully restored—it houses at its far end a collection of absurd and poetic mechanical machines invented by artist Tim Hunkin. The town also has a brewery (Adnams) established here since 1872 whose delivery drays still travel the streets by horse to preserve the cobbles.

Dunwich, ten kilometres south of Southwold, is the strangest of English villages. In the Middle Ages it was a town of 5,000 inhabitants, one of England's largest, with eight churches, two monasteries and a busy port. The sea swallowed it century by century: today, only a handful of houses remain, a small shingle beach and the ruins of a twelfth-century priory. On calm nights, fishermen off the coast claim to hear the bells of the drowned churches ringing beneath the waves.

Aldeburgh is a small fishing town made world-famous by the composer Benjamin Britten, who lived here from 1947 until his death in 1976. Born in Lowestoft in 1913, he founded the Aldeburgh Festival in 1948 with his partner, the tenor Peter Pears—the festival takes place each June and today attracts international artists. On the shingle beach, look for Maggi Hambling's Scallop sculpture, a 4.6-metre stainless steel scallop shell unveiled in 2003, engraved with words from the opera Peter Grimes. The sixteenth-century Moot Hall now stands a few metres from the sea, whereas in the sixteenth century it was in the centre of town—a measure of coastal erosion.

Eight kilometres from Aldeburgh, don't miss Snape Maltings—an ensemble of Victorian industrial buildings on the Alde estuary, transformed into one of the world's most renowned music venues. The maltings were built in 1846 by Newson Garrett, then abandoned in the 1960s. It was Britten who had the idea of converting the largest building into a concert hall: Queen Elizabeth II inaugurated it on 2 June 1967. Two years later, a fire destroyed the interior on the very evening of the festival opening. It was rebuilt in 42 weeks, and the queen came back to inaugurate it again. The 832-seat hall is now considered to have one of Europe's finest acoustics. If you don't have a concert ticket, the complex is still worth the visit—craft shops, café with views over the marshes, farmers' market at weekends.

Push on to Orford Castle, fifteen kilometres south of Aldeburgh—it's one of England's best-preserved royal castles and it's very little visited. Henry II had it built between 1165 and 1173 to assert his authority over the east of the country. Its 18-sided polygonal keep is unique in Britain. Climb to the top: the view embraces the whole of Orford Ness, a shingle spit that blocks access to the sea for fifteen kilometres—it's the largest such feature in Britain. If you have time, cross by ferry to Orford Ness itself: this secret military site abandoned in 1993 looks like an end-of-the-world scene, with its rusting bunkers lost among the shingle.

activities.text: Birdwatching at the RSPB Minsmere reserve, ten kilometres north of Aldeburgh. It's one of the UK's most important bird reserves: you regularly observe the bittern (one of Britain's rarest birds), the kingfisher and the avocet. The reserve has several hides and waymarked trails. Arrive early in the morning for the best sightings.

days_plan.itin_title: From Cambridge to London: Eastern England in 5 Days
East Anglia, Cambridge & Rutland: 13-Day Road Trip

map_keywords: Maggi Hambling Scallop sculpture Aldeburgh beach

visits.text: Alnwick Castle is England's second-largest inhabited castle — and it's still the residence of the Duke of Northumberland. Founded in 1096 by Ivo de Vesci, it has belonged to the Percy family since 1309. This is where the exterior Hogwarts scenes were filmed in the first two Harry Potter films: the inner courtyard where Harry takes his first flying lesson, the entrance to Hogwarts under the Lion Arch. The State Rooms, with gilded ceilings inspired by 16th-century Roman palaces, are open to visitors — you can feel that someone actually lives here.

The Alnwick Garden is separate from the castle with its own entrance. The Duchess of Northumberland launched its transformation in 1997. Don't miss the Poison Garden: around fifty deadly or narcotic plants, all enclosed in black railings — belladonna, mandrake, castor bean, hemlock. Guides explain the historical uses (medical, criminal, military) of each one.

A 10-minute walk from the castle, Barter Books occupies the old Victorian Alnwick Station (1887). It's one of Britain's largest secondhand bookshops: kilometers of shelving, a model train running under the ornate ceilings, and log fires in winter. This is where the original 'Keep Calm and Carry On' poster was found in 2000, in a box of old books.

activities.text: Join the castle's daily activities: medieval demonstrations, archery, and, if you fancy yourself an aspiring wizard, Broomstick Training — flying lessons take place exactly where Harry Potter filmed his first scenes.
In the evening, explore central Alnwick — a compact, lively market town whose brown sandstone architecture feels like an extension of the castle. The market runs Saturday mornings.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire and North East England: Dales, Castles and Coastal Drama
Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

visits.text: If walls could speak, those of Alton's St Lawrence's would recount 13 December 1643. Royalist Colonel Boles and his men, surrounded by Waller's Parliamentarians, held the church for two hours before being killed on the pulpit steps. The musket impacts in the south door and on the interior pillars are still there, without signage or dramatisation. Free.

days_plan.itin_title: London to Bath: Surrey, Hampshire & Wiltshire in 10 Days

visits.text: A chalk quarry in the middle of the South Downs: hard to imagine today, but this site was an active industry from 1840 to the 1960s. Amberley Museum spreads over 36 acres in the old quarries, with the De Witt lime kilns from 1905, a narrow-gauge locomotive and more than 40 exhibits on south-east England's industry.

The lime kilns at Amberley Museum still exhale a mineral smell that recalls the industrial activity of this corner of Sussex. The Pepper family ran the quarries from 1876 until closure in the 1960s. The original buildings—weighbridge office, bag shed, locomotive shed—are still standing.

activities.text: Take the narrow-gauge train around the site: a period locomotive traces a circuit of a few hundred metres through the quarries. Included in the entry price, as is the vintage bus. On-site craftsmen—blacksmith, potter, wood turner—work on certain specific days. Check the programme at amberleymuseum.co.uk.

days_plan.itin_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

places.name: Andover

visits.text: Hampshire's best local prehistory museum is in Andover—and often empty. The Museum of the Iron Age is devoted to excavations from Danebury Hill Fort, an Iron Age fortress eight kilometres away: pottery, fibulae, animal bones. Small, well-presented, free. Half an hour suffices if you're just passing through.

days_plan.itin_title: London to Bath: Surrey, Hampshire & Wiltshire in 10 Days

map_keywords: Museum of the Iron Age Andover

visits.text: Journey to South Stack Lighthouse on the island of Anglesey, perched dramatically on a rocky islet at the northwestern tip of Wales. Built in 1809 to guide ships through the treacherous waters around Holy Island, this iconic lighthouse requires a descent of 400 steps to reach, rewarding visitors with spectacular clifftop scenery along the way. The lighthouse itself can be climbed for views across to Ireland and along the rugged Anglesey coastline.

Marvel at the dramatic sea cliffs surrounding South Stack, home to thousands of seabirds including guillemots, razorbills and, during the right season, adorable puffins that nest in the rocky crevices. The RSPB nature reserve offers walking trails along the clifftops with observation points for watching the incredible birdlife and the waves crashing against the rocks far below. The wild, windswept landscape represents Wales at its most dramatically beautiful.

Explore Anglesey, the largest island in Wales, connected to the mainland by two bridges including Thomas Telford's historic suspension bridge. Beaumaris Castle, another of Edward I's mighty fortresses, represents the most technically perfect medieval castle in Britain, while the island's beaches, prehistoric sites and charming villages reward further exploration. The island's Welsh name, Ynys Môn, reflects its importance as the heartland of Welsh language and culture.

activities.text: Visit during spring or early summer for the chance to spot puffins nesting on the cliffs

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia

places.name: Arrochar

visits.text: Take time to explore Arrochar, nestled at the head of Loch Long, a sea loch stretching south to the Firth of Clyde. The name comes from the Gaelic word arachor, an old land measure equivalent to 104 acres—the area eight oxen could plow in a year. The opposite shore of the loch harbors the ruins of a former Royal Navy torpedo testing base, operational from 1912 to 1986. In 1263, Viking raiders under Haakon IV dragged their longships across the two miles separating Arrochar from Tarbet to attack the shores of Loch Lomond—before being defeated at the Battle of Largs. From the pier, the panorama over the Arrochar Alps is one of the most immediate in the Highlands.

A few minutes' drive from Arrochar, stop at the viewpoint toward Tarbet overlooking Loch Lomond & The Trossachs National Park for a first glimpse of Britain's largest freshwater lake by surface area. This national park, created in 2002, is Scotland's southernmost.

activities.text: The area's main attraction is Ben Arthur, better known as The Cobbler, a 884-meter Corbett whose three-peaked rocky summit resembles the silhouette of a cobbler bent over his work. The ascent from Succoth village (paid parking on the A83) takes between 4 and 6 hours round trip depending on your pace, with some mandatory scrambling sections to reach the true summit. If you only have half a day and moderate fitness, be content with climbing to the col for a view over Loch Long and Loch Lomond—already impressive. Bring waterproof boots and warm clothing whatever the season.

If mountain climbing isn't on the agenda, a short walk in Argyll Forest Park—Britain's first forest park—is enough to taste the Highland atmosphere. Marked trails depart from the village toward the wooded slopes overlooking the loch. Keep your eyes skyward: golden eagles regularly soar over these slopes.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye

map_keywords: Rest and Be Thankful Glen Croe

visits.text: If Stonehenge felt too controlled, too distant from the stones, here's Avebury. The world's largest stone circle is bisected by a village, its pubs and houses. You touch the megaliths, you picnic between them. The henge measures over 28 acres and includes two inner circles. UNESCO-listed alongside Stonehenge, the site dates from around 2600 BC. Alexander Keiller Museum in the village: collection of finds from the site. Circle entrance free, NT car park charges apply.

Archaeologists nicknamed it 'the avenue of lovers'—the stones of West Kennet Avenue alternate between squat (feminine) and slender (masculine) according to an old hypothesis. The avenue connects the Avebury henge to the Sanctuary site, 2.5 km to the southeast. The first 100 pairs from the original period have been partially restored. Free walk from the village.

Low, dark, cool: the burial chamber of West Kennet Long Barrow requires you to stoop to enter. The barrow dates from around 3650 BC and measures 100 metres long. Stuart Piggott's excavations (1955-56) uncovered the remains of at least 46 individuals in five chambers. The barrow was deliberately sealed around 2000 BC with large sarsen slabs. Torch recommended for the interior. Free access, 10-minute walk from Silbury Hill car park.

Thirty-nine metres of chalk piled up by hand around 2400 BC: Silbury Hill is Europe's tallest prehistoric artificial mound. Nobody knows why it was built. Nineteenth and twentieth-century excavations found no burial, no significant artefacts. It's visible from the A4 road, on-site parking, but climbing is prohibited to preserve the site. External view only.

days_plan.itin_title: Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days
London to Bath: Surrey, Hampshire & Wiltshire in 10 Days
Deep Wiltshire: from Salisbury to Newbury in 7 days

visits.text: Enter the Cairngorms National Park, Britain's largest national park, where ancient Caledonian pine forests, wild mountains and crystal-clear lochs create landscapes of extraordinary natural beauty. This wilderness in the heart of the Scottish Highlands is home to red squirrels, red deer, golden eagles and the elusive Scottish wildcat. The range of outdoor activities available here, from gentle riverside walks to challenging mountain climbs, caters to every level of adventurer.

Explore Aviemore, the adventure capital of the Cairngorms, where outdoor enthusiasts gather to pursue activities ranging from mountain biking and hiking to skiing and wildlife safaris. The town sits at the heart of the Spey Valley, surrounded by ancient forests, rushing rivers and majestic peaks. Companies offer everything from zip-lining and white water rafting to wildlife watching and wild swimming in the clear mountain waters.

Ride the Strathspey Railway, a heritage steam train that chugs through beautiful Highland scenery from Aviemore to Broomhill. This nostalgic journey in Victorian carriages offers lunch or afternoon tea while passing through landscapes of forest, moorland and mountains. The gentle pace allows time to absorb the beauty of Speyside and watch for the abundant wildlife that makes this area special.

Walk around Loch an Eilein in the ancient Rothiemurchus Forest, one of the most beautiful lochs in Scotland with a ruined castle on its island. The circular walk through Caledonian pine forest, home to red squirrels and crested tits, offers constantly changing views of the loch and surrounding mountains. The Loch an Eilein art gallery showcases local artists' works inspired by this magical landscape.

activities.text: Visit the RZSS Highland Wildlife Park near Kingussie to see polar bears, Amur tigers and Scottish wildlife including wolves and wildcats

days_plan.itin_title: Scottish Highlands Road Trip: Castles, Lochs and Wild Landscapes
Scotland: Highlands & Skye in 11 days

visits.text: Turner sketched here. Wordsworth and his sister Dorothy stopped while waiting for their stagecoach. More recently, the Robin Hood and Little John quarterstaff duel was filmed at the Upper Falls for the 1991 movie. The Ure's three waterfalls cascade over nearly 2 km of limestone slabs. The spectacle is most dramatic after rain.

activities.text: Less spectacular in dry summer, absolutely impressive after autumn or winter rains. The path linking the three falls (Upper, Middle, Lower) takes 45 minutes at a leisurely pace. The National Park Centre by the car park is free and posts current water levels.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

places.name: Bakewell

visits.text: Bakewell is the only proper town inside Peak District National Park. The Monday market is the town's oldest, active since 1254; if you're there on a weekday, the stalls of local produce and the cattle market across the river give a precise sense of what the Peak District still is: a living agricultural region. The medieval five-arched bridge over the Wye dates from the 13th century.

Don't leave without trying a Bakewell Pudding—and no, it's not the same as the Bakewell Tart found everywhere else. The real local pudding is puff pastry filled with jam and an egg-and-almond custard. Legend says it was born from a cook's mistake at the Rutland Arms around 1860: the recipe got reversed and the result was adopted by the locals. Several shops still compete today for the title of 'original recipe'.

Five kilometres from Bakewell, the village of Ashford-in-the-Water warrants 45 minutes of wandering. Sheepwash Bridge, a medieval single-arch span over the Wye, takes its name from the practice of washing sheep there before shearing. The water runs so clear you can see trout swimming below. The village itself, with its pale grey limestone houses, is the archetype of Derbyshire villages.

Chatsworth House, 5km from Bakewell on the banks of the Derwent, is one of England's grandest stately homes. The Cavendish family has lived here since 1549. Mary, Queen of Scots was imprisoned here on Elizabeth I's orders in 1569. The interiors span 4,000 years of art collection, from Roman artefacts to Old Master drawings to portraits by Lucian Freud. The house inspired the Pemberley estate in the 2005 film 'Pride & Prejudice'. The 42-hectare garden, with its cascade and Emperor Fountain (powered by gravity alone), rivals the house itself. Allow at least half a day.

Haddon Hall, 3km south of Bakewell, is one of England's best-preserved medieval manor houses. Unlike Chatsworth, it underwent no baroque or neoclassical renovation—you're visiting a 15th-century manor in near-original condition. The terraced gardens tumbling down toward the River Wye are particularly striking in June and July when in bloom.

activities.text: The Monsal Trail is a disused Victorian railway line converted into a cycling and walking trail that winds through the limestone gorges of the Wye Valley for 13km. Stone viaducts, tunnels and wooded gorges make it one of the Peak District's most spectacular non-motorised routes. Bike hire is available in Bakewell. Allow 3 hours for the return trip to Monsal Head viaduct.

days_plan.itin_title: The Midlands and Peak District Essentials
Midlands and Peak District Road Trip: Heritage and Wild Landscapes

map_keywords: Sheepwash Bridge Ashford in the Water

visits.text: Bamburgh Castle rises 45 meters above the sea on a basalt outcrop — one of England's most spectacular coastal fortresses. The site has been inhabited since the 5th century: it was the capital of the kingdom of Bernicia, the birthplace of Northumbria. The current castle dates to the 12th century and was restored in the 19th by Lord Armstrong, the Victorian industrialist. During the Wars of the Roses in 1464, it was the first English castle to surrender to gunpowder artillery — a devastating new technology. Sixteen rooms are open, including the medieval King's Hall and a museum dedicated to Armstrong.

Bamburgh Beach is one of northern England's finest: golden sand stretching for kilometers, dunes, and the castle reflected in the pools at low tide. The view to the Farne Islands on the horizon, with grey seals on the rocks, is breathtaking. It's also one of England's most photographed beaches.

The Grace Darling Museum, opposite St Aidan's Church where Grace is buried, tells the story of this Victorian heroine. In September 1838, Grace and her father — keeper of the Longstone lighthouse on the Farne Islands — took their small rowing boat into a storm to rescue nine survivors from the wreck of the SS Forfarshire. Grace was 22. Her bravery became a national sensation: the original boat is displayed in the museum.

activities.text: Take a boat from Seahouses (5 km south of Bamburgh) for a cruise to the Farne Islands. These rocky islets are home to 100,000 pairs of nesting seabirds in summer — puffins, guillemots, Arctic terns, cormorants — and one of Europe's largest grey seal colonies. Puffins, with their colorful beaks, arrive between May and July. Book ahead during high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire and North East England: Dales, Castles and Coastal Drama
Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

visits.text: Nothing remains – and that's precisely what makes the Malkin Tower site unsettling. Demdike's house, matriarch of the accused clan, was probably razed immediately after the trial to discourage gatherings. The presumed location, near Blacko, offers direct views of Pendle Hill. No official marker. The void speaks louder than any reconstruction.

activities.text: Climb to 557 metres starting from Barley village: the ascent to Pendle Hill's summit takes roughly an hour from the car park. The climb via the 'steps' – a series of cut stone blocks – is short but steep. From the summit: views across three Yorkshire Dales and, on clear days, to Blackpool Tower westward. Descend via the gentler opposite slope. Waterproof boots essential – the moorland stays waterlogged in autumn.

days_plan.itin_title: Gothic Yorkshire: Dracula, Abbeys & Witches – 8 days

visits.text: Barrowford locals know that Jennet Device was 9 years old when she testified against her mother, sister and brother at the 1612 trial. The Pendle Heritage Centre archives document this: Thomas Potts, court clerk, published everything in 1613 in The Wonderfull Discoverie of Witches. Ten hanged on 20 August 1612 at Lancaster. The centre offers the most comprehensive access to primary trial sources, with maps and chronology.

days_plan.itin_title: Gothic Yorkshire: Dracula, Abbeys & Witches – 8 days

places.name: Bath

visits.text: If you cross Exmoor in autumn, stop on the heights at sunrise: red deer stags bellow across the moorland, their antlers silhouetted in sea mist. The herd numbers around 3,000 – England's largest wild population. The Valley of Rocks, near Lynton, is a dry gorge whose pink quartzite walls host wild goats roaming free. The coastal road via Porlock Hill descends 300 meters in a few kilometers – spectacular, but take your time through the bends.

Stop at Dunster – an almost intact medieval village dominated by its castle perched on a wooded tor 60 meters high. Dunster Castle belonged to the Luttrell family for over 600 years until 1976. Most of what's visible dates from the 17th century, but seek out the 17th-century leather tapestries illustrating Antony and Cleopatra – the kind of detail you don't expect in an English country manor. On the high street, the octagonal 16th-century Yarn Market is the village centerpiece.

Bath, your overnight stop, is England's only entire UNESCO-listed city (1987). Upon arrival, walk the Royal Crescent (1767-1774) – a 114-meter curved facade designed by John Wood the Younger – and The Circus, a ring of 33 limestone houses conceived by his father in 1754 as an inverted Roman amphitheater.

Don't pass through Bath without visiting the Roman Baths – the best-preserved Roman complex north of the Alps. The King's Spring source delivers 1.2 million liters of 46°C water daily, rainwater that infiltrated 10,000 years ago and rises heated by geothermal activity. The Temple of Sulis Minerva was built around 70 CE. One thing to know: you can't bathe in the Great Bath – a bather died from an amoeba infection in 1978. To soak in the same waters, head to Thermae Bath Spa (2006) with its 40°C rooftop pool.

Pulteney Bridge, built in 1774 by Robert Adam, is one of the world's few bridges with shops along its entire length – like Florence's Ponte Vecchio. Descend to the riverbank to see it head-on – the view from below is Bath's most famous. Downstream, the cascade weirs create constant background noise.

Climb up to Prior Park, 2km from the center – the effort repays itself. This 18th-century landscape garden was designed by Ralph Allen with poet Alexander Pope's help. At the lawn's base, you'll find a Palladian bridge that's one of four replicas of this design worldwide (the other three at Stowe, Wilton, and Russia). From this bridge, the view over Bath in the valley below is unmatched – by far the best vantage point over the city.

activities.text: Crossing Exmoor, choose the coastal route via Porlock Hill. In autumn, it's rutting season: red stags bellow across the moors at dawn – an unmissable sound and sight with binoculars.

Thermae Bath Spa, opened in 2006, is the only place in the UK where you can bathe outdoors in natural thermal waters. Its rooftop pool, at 40°C, offers direct views over the Georgian skyline. The experience is particularly pleasant in winter. Book ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: England's Gastronomic Trail: Markets, Pubs & Culinary Heritage
England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes
England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes
Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days
London to Bath: Surrey, Hampshire & Wiltshire in 10 Days
London to Bath: Surrey, Hampshire & Wiltshire in 10 Days

map_keywords: Exmoor National Park Valley of Rocks

places.name: Battle

visits.text: On 14 October 1066, on this bare hilltop, two armies met and England changed language. William built Battle Abbey in penance for the slaughter—the high altar was placed at the exact spot where Harold died. A slab still marks the position in the grass. The free audio guide walks the battlefield with wooden markers.

Battle's High Street, medieval façades too quiet for a town that gave its name to a decisive battle. Walking up towards the abbey, sixteenth-century timber-framed fronts follow the shops. Battle also became, in the seventeenth century, England's main centre of gunpowder production—supplying the army until the Crimean War.

activities.text: Follow the audio guide on the Battle Abbey battlefield: it reconstructs the Norman advance, the Saxon resistance and Harold's death. The visitor centre's interactive panels include CGI reconstructions. During the annual October re-enactment weekend, more than 500 participants recreate the fighting on the original site.

days_plan.itin_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

map_keywords: Battle Abbey East Sussex English Heritage

visits.text: If an unfinished castle can be a masterpiece, it's Beaumaris. Edward I and his architect James of St George laid out their most ambitious fortress here in 1295, the last of the iron ring, designed from an empty marsh without the constraints of a pre-existing site. Result: near-perfect concentric symmetry, 300 arrow slits total, moats with their own dock. The money ran out, Scotland called, the towers were never completed—and UNESCO inscribed it anyway in 1986.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

places.name: Beckenham

visits.text: Bedlam, a name that became an insult, remains Europe's oldest psychiatric hospital, founded in 1247 by Simon FitzMary in Bishopsgate. The museum, opened in 2015 in an Art Deco building, displays archives, straitjackets, 19th-century restraint instruments, and an outsider art collection by patients. Free entry, Wednesday to Saturday. From Victoria, train to Beckenham Junction, 25 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Dark London: 6 days on the Shadow Side

map_keywords: Bethlem Museum of the Mind Beckenham

visits.text: Painted on the gable end of Sinn Féin headquarters at the corner of Falls Road and Sevastopol Street, Bobby Sands' smiling face is the most photographed mural in Belfast. The artwork dates from 1998 and reproduces a photo taken at Long Kesh, before the hunger strike that killed him in 1981 at age 27. All around, the International Wall rotates through solidarity murals: Palestine, Cuba, American civil rights. Walk early morning when the light is better and the tourist pickpockets still asleep.

One hundred metres on foot, world turned inside out. Shankill Road is the Protestant-loyalist artery running parallel to Falls Road. UVF and UDA flags, murals of armed paramilitaries, flowers on pavements for the dead from the other side. The frontier is invisible but it's everywhere: pubs, schools, shops, everything doubled. This is Belfast's most unsettling experience and the most useful for understanding the Good Friday Agreement.

Eight metres of concrete, wire mesh on top to block projectiles. The Cupar Way Peace Wall was started by the British Army on 10 September 1969, after the August riots. It was meant to be temporary. Fifty-seven years later it's still here, gates still locked at night. Bring a marker if you want to add a word. Aim for late afternoon, low sun on the graffiti.

The tunnel still smells of damp limestone. Running beneath Crumlin Road, it links the Victorian prison to the courthouse opposite—prisoners crossed it in handcuffs until 1996. The Crum opened in 1846, closed a century and a half later. Seventeen men were hanged here between 1854 and 1961, the last being Robert McGladdery. C-wing held republican and loyalist prisoners during the Troubles. Guided tour runs 70 to 90 minutes, take the guided rather than the audio.

Slightly pompous but classy Edwardian building, City Hall was completed in 1906 on Donegall Square. The dome is visible from all over the city centre. In the gardens east of the facade, the Titanic Memorial Garden lists the names of the 1517 victims of the 1912 sinking, many of them Belfast workers. Free guided tour at 11am and 2pm on weekdays, it's short and well done.

If you're wary of tourist traps, forget your prejudices. Titanic Belfast sits on the old Harland & Wolff shipyards, right where the hull was launched on 31 May 1911 in exactly 62 seconds. Nine galleries, including a mini-ride that passes through a reconstruction of the shipyards. The museum opened in 2012 for the centenary of the sinking. Allow 2h30 and book online, morning slots go first.

Red-and-black hull moored next to the museum, SS Nomadic is the last White Star Line vessel still afloat. Built in 1911 in the same yards, she ferried first-class passengers to Titanic at Cherbourg, the port being too small to accommodate the giant. Restored in 2013. Thirty minutes is enough, combined ticket with Titanic Belfast works out cheaper.

activities.text: To understand both sides, take a black cab tour with an ex-prisoner guide. Coiste runs pairings of loyalist and republican guides who tell the story of the same street from two angles. Expect £25 to £40 per person for two hours, tip customary. Book the day before in high season, the best guides go fast.

For dinner, the Mourne Seafood Bar on Bank Street is a safe bet: Carlingford oysters, Donegal mussels, simplicity. Book two days ahead, especially Friday and Saturday. Expect £30 to £40 per person with a drink.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Ireland in 7 days: Belfast, Causeway & Derry
Northern Ireland in 7 days: Belfast, Causeway & Derry

visits.text: This is where it happened: at Berkhamsted Castle, William the Conqueror accepted the submission of England's lords in 1066—mere weeks after Hastings. What remains is a motte-and-bailey ruin (11th-15th century), but the site's logic is still clear: the motte stands distinct, the ditches intact. Free entry, open year-round. Avoid weekend middays if you're after solitude.

The scent of old Berkhamsted town hits as soon as you leave the castle: fresh bread, hops, warm brick. The High Street retains several Georgian and Tudor buildings. St Peter's Church is the oldest in town, with parts dating to the 12th century. No formal museum, but the urban fabric rewards a wander.

activities.text: Stroll Berkhamsted's High Street, which holds several old cafés and pubs. The Grand Junction Arms, near the canal, offers a pleasant terrace along the Grand Union Canal—15 minutes' walk from the castle.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Locals simply call it 'the gateway to Snowdonia' and the name says it all: Betws-y-Coed (pronounced 'bett-us-uh-koyd') is the slate-roofed village nestled at the confluence of four rivers where every hiker ends up sooner or later. The Welsh name means 'prayer house in the forest'. The Pont-y-Pair bridge, built around 1500, withstands the Llugwy floods with almost arrogant presence. Visit the Railway Museum for children and scout hiking routes at the information centre.

activities.text: Take the A5 west for 3 miles to reach Swallow Falls, where the River Llugwy tumbles 42 metres down a narrow limestone gorge. The falls are among the highest in North Wales; the swifts that inspired their name swoop in June and July. Access to the viewing platform costs a few pounds at the turnstile. Waterproof shoes strongly recommended.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

visits.text: Spurn Point is Yorkshire's end of the world. This sand-and-shingle spit stretches 5 kilometres between the North Sea and the Humber estuary—in places barely 50 metres wide. In December 2013, a storm destroyed the road that crossed it: since then, the tip has become an island at high tide, accessible only to walkers and cyclists. The Yorkshire Wildlife Trust runs a safari in a converted military Unimog—two hours through the dunes to the 1895 lighthouse, First World War blockhouses, and abandoned lifeboat crew houses. Check tide times before you go—the washover section can cut off access.

Spurn Lighthouse, restored in 2015 after decades of abandonment, rises 39 metres and offers 360° views over the estuary and the North Sea. It's a Victorian structure from 1895—Yorkshire Wildlife Trust volunteers recount the history of the lifeboat station that operated here without interruption from 1810, with permanent crews and their families living at the tip of this sand tongue. The last family left in 2012.

Beverley Minster announces itself the moment you turn into Minster Yard: twin limestone towers rising over 40 metres, a west facade so perfect in its proportions that Nicholas Hawksmoor used it as the model for Westminster Abbey's towers. Inside, the Minster stretches 122 metres—longer than many English cathedrals—and houses 68 16th-century misericords, the largest collection of any parish church in the country. Don't miss the carved scene of the fox preaching to geese, and geese hanging the fox—medieval satire was unsparing. Construction began in 1220, finished in 1425, with three Gothic styles layered atop one another. Free entry.

St Mary's Church, Beverley, a ten-minute walk away, gets fewer visitors but is nearly as beautiful—its 15th-century painted ceiling depicts the kings of England from the Middle Ages onward. Lewis Carroll, a frequent visitor to Beverley, supposedly found inspiration for the White Rabbit here: a rabbit carrying a watch appears carved in one of the vaults.

Beverley Westwood is a vast common at the edge of town where cattle have grazed freely since the 12th century—a grazing right codified by royal charter. It's also the locals' playground: golf, running, picnics. A half-hour walk from the centre to decompress before hitting the road.

activities.text: The walk from the visitor centre to the tip of Spurn runs about 10 kilometres over sand and shingle—allow three hours. You'll pass the ruins of a Great War coastal battery, shipwrecks half-buried in sand, and in autumn thousands of Scandinavian migrant birds stopping here. Bring binoculars.

Late afternoon, head north to Beverley for the night—50 minutes through the Holderness plains. The old town warrants an evening stroll around North Bar, England's only surviving medieval fortified gateway in a town of this size, dating from 1409.

Beverley's Saturday Market—or any weekday market in the town centre—is the beating heart of the town. Beverley has exported wool to Ghent and Ypres since the 12th century, and its agricultural market is one of Yorkshire's oldest. Local cheeses, craft beers, independent butchers and cheesemongers holding the line against supermarkets.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands
Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands

visits.text: Arlington Row dates from 1380, built as a wool store for a monastery, converted to weavers' cottages in the late 17th century. The row appeared on the inside cover of British passports (between 2010 and 2015). William Morris called Bibury the most beautiful village in England. Henry Ford once wanted to buy Arlington Row and dismantle it for shipping to the United States—refused.

activities.text: If you arrive in Bibury after 10am in season, the car park is saturated and Arlington Row cottages are photographed over tourists' heads. Come before 9am or late in the day. The walk along the Coln, behind the main street, takes you out of selfie range in two minutes. Bibury Trout Farm sells smoked trout to take away, ideal for a picnic.

days_plan.itin_title: Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days

visits.text: Look up the moment you approach Parliament: the Elizabeth Tower - which everyone calls Big Ben - soars to 96 metres. Big Ben is actually the name of the 13.7-tonne bell that first chimed on 11 July 1859, only to crack a few months later. Engineers simply rotated it 90 degrees and reduced the hammer size - and it's been ringing ever since. Each clock face measures 7 metres in diameter and comprises 312 pieces of glass. Since 2023, overseas visitors can book guided tours to climb the 334 steps to the belfry - places sell out fast, so book well ahead on the UK Parliament website.

From Westminster Bridge, you get the best overall view of the Elizabeth Tower with the Thames in the foreground. It's the classic starting point for photographing the tower with a red double-decker bus - arrive early morning to avoid the crowds. At night, the Gothic facade of the Palace of Westminster lights up in gold, creating one of the city's most beautiful nocturnal panoramas.

activities.text: Walk along the north bank of the Thames from Westminster Bridge towards Westminster Pier. You can board a river bus there to reach the South Bank or Tower Bridge in minutes - a far more pleasant way than the Tube to move between riverside sites.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Drop your bags and head straight down to Gas Street Basin - this is where Birmingham shows its best face as evening sets in. The basin is the heart of the city's canal network, 35 miles of waterways dug from 1768 onwards to carry coal and iron from the factories. More than Venice's 26 miles, which Brummies have been boasting about for two centuries. Today the narrowboats have made way for terrace tables, but the evening atmosphere is excellent - lights reflecting on the water, the sound of glasses clinking, the smell of cooking. Start here.

Birmingham Museum and Art Gallery, opened in 1885, holds the world's largest public collection of Pre-Raphaelite works - over 2,000 pieces including Burne-Jones, Rossetti, and Ford Madox Brown covering entire walls. The collection has just been reinstalled in four completely renovated galleries after years of closure. What's remarkable here is that entry is free. Allow at least 2 hours, and make sure to see the Staffordshire Hoard: the largest Anglo-Saxon gold and silver treasure ever discovered, hundreds of pieces found in a Staffordshire field in 2009.

The Jewellery Quarter deserves a good half-day. This district still produces 40% of the jewelry made in the United Kingdom and has been designated a World Craft City by the World Crafts Council - one of only eight places in Europe to receive this distinction. Walk up Vyse Street and Warstone Lane: goldsmith workshops sit alongside galleries and independent boutiques. St Paul's Square, the city's last intact Georgian square, is the perfect spot for a pause in the sun. If you have time, the Museum of the Jewellery Quarter - a former factory left untouched since its closure - offers 45-minute guided tours where you can still see tools on the workbenches. Book ahead for weekends.

Aston Hall is worth the trip for its history alone: this Jacobean mansion built between 1618 and 1635 received Charles I in 1642, the night before he left to fight at Edgehill - one of the first battles of the English Civil War. The king slept here. Today managed by Birmingham Museums, the rooms have remained virtually unchanged since the 17th century. It's one of the few interiors from this period still accessible to the public in England.

Digbeth, east of the center, is Birmingham's creative quarter - Victorian warehouses converted into galleries, music studios, and street art spaces. The Custard Factory, a former Bird's factory that made custard powder, has become the symbol of this transformation: independent shops, cafes, artist workshops. If you like this kind of place, set aside the afternoon here and explore on foot.

The Thinktank Birmingham Science Museum, in the former Curzon Street goods station - the world's oldest surviving railway station building - offers exhibitions on the industrial history of the Midlands. It's both an interactive science museum and a tribute to the ingenuity that made the city's reputation. Ideal if you're traveling with children, or simply if you like machines.

activities.text: Climb aboard a narrowboat from Gas Street Basin to see Birmingham from the water. It's the most surprising way to understand why this city dominated the global industrial revolution - from the canal, you can still see Victorian warehouses, cast-iron bridges, and locks unchanged for 200 years. Rental companies offer boats by the hour or day, no license required.

In the evening, stay in the Jewellery Quarter rather than heading down to Broad Street, which is more touristy. The bars around St Paul's Square have a local crowd, a more relaxed atmosphere, and the prices reflect that. If you like whisky, the Grain & Glass on Frederick Street keeps a cellar of over 300 single malts available for tasting.

Six kilometers south of the center, the village of Bournville is a unique Victorian urban planning experiment: chocolatier Cadbury had it built from 1879 onwards to house his workers in better conditions than the industrial slums of the time. Cadbury World traces the entire family history and chocolate-making process, with tastings. Avoid the weekend - queues are long, weekdays are better.

If you're there on a Tuesday, Friday, or Saturday, don't miss Birmingham's indoor market in Digbeth. It's one of England's oldest, and it gives a very accurate picture of working-class Birmingham - textiles, spices, local producers. Not at all touristy, and far more interesting than the Bullring.

days_plan.itin_title: The Midlands and Peak District Essentials
Midlands and Peak District Road Trip: Heritage and Wild Landscapes
Midlands and Peak District Road Trip: Heritage and Wild Landscapes
Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days

visits.text: A museum dedicated to Cecil Rhodes might seem out of place in a Hertfordshire market town—except Rhodes was born here in 1853, in the house that now holds Bishop's Stortford Museum. His collection of South African artifacts and life archives remain. The museum changed names after debates over his colonial legacy, but the collection stays, with updated contextualization.

The Norman motte of Waytemore Castle dominates Castle Gardens right in Bishop's Stortford's center. Only the earth mound and foundation traces remain, but the landscaped gardens below make a worthwhile stop.

activities.text: Walk down the River Stort from Bishop's Stortford on the towpath to Hockerill Road Bridge—25 minutes round trip, flat. The willow-lined river is especially beautiful in spring.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Bodiam Castle reflected in its moat: too perfect, you'd think it was a set. The Monty Python and the Holy Grail crew certainly thought so, using it briefly for an exterior shot of Swamp Castle. Built in 1385 by Sir Edward Dalyngrigge, knight of Edward III, to defend the Rother valley against the French during the Hundred Years' War. The exterior is almost intact; the interior is a roofless ruin open to the wind.

Built in 1385 by Sir Edward Dalyngrigge, knight of Edward III, Bodiam is a courtyard castle: four round corner towers, two gates framed by rectangular towers. The keyhole-shaped arrow slits bear witness to the use of early portable firearms. The moat was drained during excavations in 1970.

activities.text: Take the Kent & East Sussex Railway from Tenterden if you missed it yesterday—the line stops a stone's throw from the castle. Otherwise, the walk from the car park follows the Rother bank for half an hour. The approach on foot is far better: the castle appears suddenly above the reeds.

days_plan.itin_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

visits.text: Explore the wild expanses of Bodmin Moor, a vast inland moorland of granite outcrops and barren pastures that stands apart from Cornwall's famous coastline. This ancient landscape is recognized as one of the finest locations in Europe for Neolithic and Bronze Age monuments, with stone circles, standing stones and burial chambers scattered across the heather-covered hills. The mysterious King Arthur's Hall, a megalithic ceremonial site, is among the most famous prehistoric remains.

Discover the fascinating geological history of this wild place, where weathered tors rise from the moorland like sentinels from another age. The landscape has inspired legends and folklore for millennia, from tales of the Beast of Bodmin to Arthurian mythology. Walking trails crisscross the moor, offering solitude and dramatic scenery far removed from the coastal crowds.

activities.text: Hike to Brown Willy, the highest point in Cornwall, for panoramic views across the moorland

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide
Dark Cornwall: Arthur, Mines and Wreckers 7d

visits.text: The moor weighs on your shoulders the moment you leave the A30. Jamaica Inn, built in 1750 as a coaching house, was immortalized in 1936 by Daphne du Maurier after she got lost here on horseback with a friend in November 1930—their horses led them back alone through the misty night. Smugglers, claimed ghosts, a museum of maritime poaching. Stop in for a pint even if you're not staying overnight.

days_plan.itin_title: Dark Cornwall: Arthur, Mines and Wreckers 7d

visits.text: Discover Bournemouth, the largest seaside town in Dorset and voted the happiest place in the UK, where award-winning Blue Flag beaches stretch for miles beneath dramatic cliffs. This vibrant coastal resort boasts Green Flag gardens that make up an impressive 20% of the entire town, creating a unique blend of seaside excitement and natural tranquility. The Victorian pier, colorful beach huts and golden sands have drawn visitors since the town's founding in the 19th century.

Stroll through Bournemouth's magnificent gardens, starting with the Lower, Central and Upper Gardens in the heart of town, home to plants and trees from across the globe. The Alum Chine Tropical Gardens transport visitors to the Mediterranean with their exotic plantings, while Pinecliff Gardens offer Spanish-style landscapes overlooking the sea. Stour Valley provides a peaceful village atmosphere with the calming sounds of the flowing River Stour.

Visit the Russell-Cotes Art Gallery and Museum, a stunning Victorian mansion perched on the East Cliff that houses an eclectic collection of Victorian and Edwardian art and artifacts. The building itself is a work of art, with ornate interiors and breathtaking views across the bay. Pay your respects at St Peter's Church, the final resting place of Frankenstein author Mary Shelley, whose grave draws literary pilgrims from around the world.

activities.text: Walk from Bournemouth Pier to Boscombe Pier, discovering historical displays showing how seaside life has evolved over the decades

days_plan.itin_title: Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks
England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton

visits.text: Bourton calls itself the Venice of the Cotswolds. It's an exaggeration for five stone bridges over a shallow stream, but the overall picture works. The Windrush winds past low cottages, you sit beside it with an ice cream watching the ducks. Touristy in July-August, peaceful from late afternoon once the coaches leave.

activities.text: If you push through to Bourton at 4pm, you'll find a different village: the coaches depart, the locals reappear. The Model Village (a 1/9-scale replica of Bourton built in 1937) holds a child's attention for 30 minutes. Birdland, further on, has penguins and pelicans. Otherwise, walk to Lower Slaughter on the footpath, 1.5 km, quieter than the main village.

days_plan.itin_title: Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days

places.name: Brecon

visits.text: Pen y Fan is the highest peak in southern Britain—886 metres. From its flat ridge, on a clear day, the view sweeps across the entire Gower Peninsula and the Bristol Channel. Its Welsh name literally means 'the summit of the peak', and it was once known as Cadair Arthur—Arthur's Chair. The path from the Pont ar Daf car park (on the A470) is the most direct: 3.2 km one way, 450 metres of ascent. It's the 'motorway' that regulars love to mock, but the summit view is worth any crowd. Arrive before 9am in summer if you want the ridge to yourself.

If you tackle the Pen y Fan Horseshoe, the summit of Cribyn (795 m) comes naturally into the itinerary: it overlooks Pen y Fan from its eastern ridge and offers a different angle—more open towards the Neuadd Reservoir and the central hills. The ridge between the two summits is narrow in places; beware of gusts. Bring waterproofs and proper boots whatever the forecast in Brecon below—conditions change fast at 800 metres.

Brecon is a pleasant Georgian market town, set in the Usk Valley between the Brecon Beacons and the Black Mountains. Wander around the 11th-century cathedral and the Monmouth & Brecon Canal—one of Wales' best-preserved canals, where you can hire a boat for the day.

Waterfall Country, between Pontneddfechan and Ystradfellte, packs more waterfalls per square kilometre than anywhere else in Britain. The Mellte and Hepste rivers plunge through wooded gorges carved into red sandstone, creating a succession of falls each with its own character. Pontneddfechan is the best entry point for walkers coming from the south—the Elidir Trail starts here, and the toilets and café at the Waterfall Centre are in the village.

Sgwd yr Eira—'the fall of snow' in Welsh—is the undisputed star of the Four Waterfalls Walk. Nine metres wide, it drops in an almost horizontal curtain over the River Hepste, and a narrow path cut into the rock lets you walk literally behind the veil of water. This path was used by shepherds to get their flocks across to the other side. Behind the fall, the noise is deafening, the ground slick and slippery, the air cold and saturated with droplets—you see daylight filtered through the water curtain like through frosted glass. Prepare to get drenched even in fine weather, put your phone in a waterproof pocket before approaching, and wear grip-soled shoes. The descent from the main path involves about 170 steep steps.

Henrhyd Falls, about fifteen kilometres further west near Coelbren, is the tallest waterfall in South Wales—27 metres. It's less visited than the Four Waterfalls and worth the detour if you have time. Access from the National Trust car park takes 20 minutes on foot.

The village of Ystradfellte is the ideal starting point for exploring the eastern part of Waterfall Country. Tiny—a handful of farms and an inn—but well positioned between Sgwd Clun-Gwyn, Sgwd Isaf Clun-Gwyn and Sgwd yr Eira. The Cwm Porth car park (2 km south) gives access to the Four Waterfalls loop.

activities.text: For a shorter, quieter ascent, the Cwm Gwdi car park (north side) offers a more intimate approach to Pen y Fan via the Cefn Cwm Llwch ridge: fewer people, spectacular views over the glacial valley and a cirque lake. The loop is about 8.5 km with 545 m of ascent—allow 3 to 4 hours.

The Brecon Mountain Railway runs from Merthyr Tydfil up to Pontsticill Reservoir across the moorland—a 7 km journey by steam train with views of Pen y Fan. Ideal if your legs are tired after the morning's hike. Check the timetable before you go.

The Blaen-y-Glyn waterfalls, in Talybont Forest, are among the least visited in the Brecon Beacons—which is precisely their charm. A series of short falls cascade through a wooded ravine, reached in 20 minutes from the forest car park. Perfect for a late afternoon after the morning's effort.

The Four Waterfalls Walk is a 7 km loop linking Sgwd Clun-Gwyn, Sgwd Isaf Clun-Gwyn, Sgwd y Pannwr and Sgwd yr Eira. The route is well marked but the descents to the waterfalls are steep, muddy and slippery—proper walking boots are essential. Allow between 2h30 and 4h depending on your pace. The waterfalls are most impressive in spring and autumn after rain; in summer, the flow can be low.

The Elidir Trail loop from Pontneddfechan combines the path along the River Neath with the crossing of the four waterfalls—a more ambitious version (12 km) that encounters fewer people and offers viewpoints from the ridges. This loop starts and ends at the Waterfall Centre in Pontneddfechan, where a café is available.

days_plan.itin_title: South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast
South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast
Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

map_keywords: Pont ar Daf car park Brecon Beacons
Brecknock Museum and Art Gallery Brecon

visits.text: Brightlingsea is the only Cinque Ports member located outside Kent and Sussex—a medieval status that conferred trading and military privileges. Brightlingsea Museum, reopened in 2020 in modern premises, tells this unusual maritime history with well-designed exhibitions.
Brightlingsea Museum, reopened in 2020, gives a clear picture of an Essex port's life from Roman times to today. The room on the Cinque Ports and medieval trade is particularly well documented.

activities.text: Take the ferry from Brightlingsea Harbour to reach East Mersea and Cudmore Grove Country Park. Service runs April to mid-September. The crossing takes a few minutes and arrival on a natural beach is striking.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Beachy Head is the highest point of southern England's cliffs: 162 meters of pure chalk in sheer overhang above the Channel. The name comes from the old French Beauchef, which appears in texts from 1274—so it has nothing to do with a beach. The close-cropped grass at the summit is deceptively gentle: don't approach within 9 meters of the edge. On clear days, the view extends to the French coast.

The Seven Sisters are the seven white cliff sisters stretching west from Birling Gap for about 8 km. Haven Brow, the highest, reaches 77 meters. Their dazzling whiteness is due to continuous erosion—unlike Dover's cliffs, protected and overgrown with green vegetation, these remain pristine white. Their sawtooth profile was nicknamed by 18th-century sailors a line of seven maidens lifting their petticoats. The view from Seaford Head, on the other side of the Cuckmere estuary, is the most photographed. Bring walking shoes.

The Royal Pavilion is one of England's most improbable architectural curiosities. The Prince of Wales—future George IV—discovered Brighton in 1783 and had a seaside residence built starting in 1787. In 1815, he commissioned architect John Nash for a radical transformation: the result is a palace with domes and minarets of Mughal inspiration, entirely in cast-iron facade, unique in the country. The interior is even more spectacular: the banqueting room is adorned with golden dragons, the music room has a ceiling composed of 26,000 gilded scales, and the ultra-modern kitchen for its time had automatic spits and heated tables. Queen Victoria detested the curiosity of Brighton residents and sold the palace to the city in 1850 for £53,000—it was a bargain. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

Brighton Palace Pier, inaugurated in 1899, is Victorian England's liveliest pier. Don't expect anything subdued: rides, slot machine arcades, and chips—but the experience is authentically British and the view from the pier's end over the city and pebbles is worthwhile.

The Lanes—a labyrinth of medieval alleys winding through the heart of old Brighton—house jewelry shops, antique dealers, and galleries in 15th- and 16th-century timber-framed houses. This is where Brighton fishermen lived before the town became a fashionable seaside resort. Just to the north, the North Laine district offers a more bohemian register: vinyl, vintage clothing, independent cafés.

activities.text: Hike the South Downs Way between Birling Gap and Beachy Head: allow 2 to 3 hours for this 8 km section with regular elevation changes. On the return, the terrace at the National Trust café at Birling Gap is perfect for soup and a sandwich facing the cliffs. Outside summer, local buses connect Eastbourne to Birling Gap—check schedules.

Brighton is England's capital of alternative pubs, bars, and clubs. In the evening, the area between the station and the sea comes alive until very late. If you prefer something quieter, the Brighton Museum and Art Gallery (in the Pavilion's former royal stables) has excellent Art Nouveau and Art Deco collections, as well as a fascinating selection of Egyptian objects.

days_plan.itin_title: Southeast England & Kent Circuit: White Cliffs and Secret Gardens
Southeast England & Kent Circuit: White Cliffs and Secret Gardens
England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton

visits.text: St Nicholas Market, or St Nicks to Bristolians, has been open since 1743 and gathers the city's largest collection of independent traders beneath its covered halls. It's divided into three sections—the Glass Arcade, the Covered Market and the Exchange—with over 60 stalls. If you're there on a Friday, the open-air street food market on Wine Street is an unmissable appointment. Inside, look for Pieminister for artisan beef or veggie pies, and stalls selling local Somerset cheeses.

The Clifton neighbourhood, just above the centre, is home to several quality delicatessens and cheese shops. Papadeli on Whiteladies Road is a reference point for cakes, salads and its cheese counter. If you're there on a Saturday morning, Whiteladies Farmers' Market is where the city's chefs stock up on local produce.

activities.text: Bristol has several active gin distilleries in the city centre. Psychopomp, housed in a former grocer's on St Michael's Hill, is one of the best known and offers tasting workshops and 'create your own gin' masterclasses. Six O'Clock Gin, larger, has a distillery in Thornbury with guided tours, and a bar-shop on The Glassboat beside the Avon. Bristol's gin offering is serious—don't leave without a bottle.

Several award-winning gastropubs occupy Bristol's streets. Bristol Lido, wrapped around its heated Victorian pool, is probably the city's most original restaurant: you can swim before dining in magnificent surroundings. The menu relies on local produce from Somerset and Devon. Book your dinner—and if you're tempted, the Swim & Dine package includes pool, spa and restaurant.

days_plan.itin_title: England's Gastronomic Trail: Markets, Pubs & Culinary Heritage
Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days
England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton

visits.text: The British Library, opened in its current building near King's Cross in 1998 after 15 years of construction, is the largest public building erected in the UK in the 20th century. It holds over 170 million items on 625 km of shelving and receives a copy of every publication produced in the UK and Ireland—roughly 3 million additions per year. For visitors, one room is essential: the Treasures Gallery (free admission), where you'll find two of the four original copies of the 1215 Magna Carta, a 1455 Gutenberg Bible (one of 49 surviving copies), the 8th-century Lindisfarne Gospels, the Diamond Sutra from 868 AD (the oldest known printed book), the 4th-century Codex Sinaiticus, Shakespeare's First Folio from 1623, and the lyrics to 'Yesterday' scribbled by Paul McCartney on an envelope. The room is kept in low light to protect these treasures—allow 1.5 to 2 hours to properly view everything.

activities.text: If you're interested in research, request a reader's pass to access the reading rooms. Otherwise, the museum shop offers high-quality reproductions of certain manuscripts.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: The British Museum opened in 1759 - it's the world's first public national museum. Its 8 million objects span two million years of human history. The Rosetta Stone, its most visited object, is displayed in Room 4: carved in 196 BC, it bears the same decree in hieroglyphs, Demotic and Greek. It was this parallel that enabled Champollion to decipher hieroglyphs in 1822, opening ancient Egypt to modern understanding. It arrived in London in 1802 after French capitulation in Egypt - its potential return to Egypt is an active debate. Admission is free, plan at least 2.5 hours.

The Great Court, covered by a glass dome designed by Norman Foster and opened in 2000, is Europe's largest covered public square. It surrounds the circular Reading Room from 1857, where Marx, Lenin and Darwin worked. The north wing houses the Greek rooms with the Parthenon friezes, which Greece has been claiming back since 1983.

activities.text: If the collections are too vast to digest alone, several operators offer themed guided tours of 1.5 to 2 hours concentrating on the 20 most important works. The museum app offers free self-guided audio tours in several languages. The Bloomsbury neighbourhood around the museum deserves a walk - its 18th-century Georgian squares are among London's finest.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries
What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Buckingham Palace became the official royal residence in 1837 under Queen Victoria - before her, sovereigns lodged at St James's Palace. The East facade you see from The Mall was redesigned in 1913 by Sir Aston Webb, along with the Queen Victoria Memorial in front of the gates. In summer (generally July to September), the State Rooms open to the public - book online, morning slots are less crowded.

activities.text: The Changing of the Guard takes place on Mondays, Wednesdays, Fridays and Sundays at 11am on the Palace forecourt, for a ceremony lasting about 45 minutes. Arrive 60 to 90 minutes early for a good spot - the steps of the Victoria Memorial offer the best elevated view. The ceremony can be cancelled in heavy rain, so check the official calendar that morning on the Household Division website.

Cross St James's Park towards Trafalgar Square via Birdcage Walk. This 23-hectare royal park offers an unusual view of Buckingham Palace from the central bridge - with the lawns and lake, it's one of the best vantage points for the Palace.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: If the church is open, step inside: the medieval glass shields lined up in the chancel are among the oldest surviving stained glass in this part of historic Berkshire. Village of a few houses, church rarely locked, free entry. The 12th-century nave and Early English chancel (around 1240) form a sober, intact ensemble.

days_plan.itin_title: Thames & Vale of White Horse: 6 days Loop

places.name: Burford

visits.text: The High Street slopes down to the Windrush, lining up buildings from the 15th century to Georgian fronts almost without a break. This is what they call the 'gateway to the Cotswolds'. St John the Baptist Church, founded in 1175, keeps bullet holes in the wall from 1649: Cromwell had imprisoned 340 Leveller mutineers there, executing three of them in the churchyard.

activities.text: The English pronounce Burford 'burr-furd', not 'byoo-furd'. The High Street is one of the few to earn the label 'finest in England' without blushing: 800 metres of unbroken period facades. The Royal Oak does game pies worth the stop. The Tolsey Museum, in the hall above the pillars, is free and tells the town's story in 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days

map_keywords: St John the Baptist Church Burford

visits.text: Norwich was England's second city after London at the time of the Norman Conquest—that's why William the Conqueror immediately built a castle here. The current castle, with its Victorian facade (19th-century cladding replaced the original Norman stone), is now an excellent museum covering regional history, the Iceni and Boudica, the Vikings, and Norfolk arts. Take time to explore the dungeons and galleries.

Elm Hill is Norwich's most complete medieval street, and one of England's finest. Nearly all the buildings you see were constructed after the 1507 fire that ravaged over 700 houses in the city—only Briton's Arms, a thatched inn dating from 1347, survived the flames. Walking up the cobbled street, glance at the blue plaque of Pettus House (1550) and the stairs of merchants' houses whose backs gave directly onto the River Wensum.

Norwich Cathedral is one of England's great medieval cathedrals, begun in 1096 by Bishop Herbert de Losinga. Its 96-metre spire is surpassed only by Salisbury. It houses the world's largest collection of carved medieval vault bosses—over 1,000 painted bosses adorn nave and cloisters. The cloisters, England's largest, are worth a good half-hour alone. During summer, Shakespeare plays are performed here.

Holkham Beach is breathtaking—at low tide, an expanse of white, smooth sand opens as far as the eye can see, framed by pine forests planted in the late 19th century by the 2nd Earl of Leicester to protect farmland from wind. It was in this golden light that Gwyneth Paltrow walked during the final scenes of Shakespeare in Love (1998). Holkham National Nature Reserve covers 3,900 hectares and welcomes large formations of pink-footed geese in winter. Arrive early in summer.

Holkham Hall is a Palladian mansion built between 1734 and 1764 by Thomas Coke, 1st Earl of Leicester, to designs by William Kent. The marble hall with its 15-metre pressed-stucco dome is one of 18th-century England's finest interiors. The state rooms house canvases by Rubens, Van Dyck, Claude Lorrain and Gainsborough. The 25,000-acre park with its lake and red deer can be visited by bike or on foot.

Wells-next-the-Sea is an active port with a colourful quay lined with fishing boats and smoked fish shops. The town added 'next-the-sea' to its name in the 19th century to distinguish itself from other Wells. Stroll along the quay, climb to the beach huts at the end of the embankment, and listen for the tide siren—a wartime relic that still sounds to warn bathers when the sea rises.

Walsingham is a pilgrimage village active since the Middle Ages—its 12th-century Augustinian priory ruins and Catholic and Anglican shrines make it one of England's most important Marian devotion sites. To reach it from Wells, take the Wells & Walsingham Light Railway, a 4-mile narrow-gauge steam train—the longest railway of this gauge in England.

Blakeney is one of Norfolk's most beautiful coastal villages, with its flint houses and quay opening onto an estuary of great beauty. At low tide, the harbour empties almost completely, leaving boats stranded on mudflats. Climb to St Nicholas church, built in 13th-century flint brick with a secondary tower that served as a lighthouse for sailors.

Cley next the Sea, just east of Blakeney, has a 17th-century windmill on the edge of the marshes that has become the photographic symbol of North Norfolk. The mouth of the River Glaven and surrounding marshes are a premier bird reserve—bring binoculars.

activities.text: Late afternoon, arrive in Burnham Market to explore the village. This 'Chelsea of Norfolk' as locals call it is organized around a long grassy green flanked by Georgian brick houses. The independent shops, galleries and pubs make it a pleasant stop for an evening. It's an excellent base for the next two days on Norfolk's north coast.
Walk along the North Norfolk coast from Burnham Market

The boat trip to Blakeney Point to observe grey and common seals is one of Norfolk's most popular nature outings. The sand spit of Blakeney Point, managed by the National Trust since 1912, hosts one of Britain's largest grey seal colonies. Trips depart from Blakeney quay and last about 1.5 hours. Book ahead in high season.

The North Norfolk coast is best walked on the Norfolk Coast Path. From Burnham Market, the section to Holkham or Wells offers magnificent views over salt marshes and dunes. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a loop.

East Anglia Circuit: Cambridge, Suffolk Coast and Royal Norfolk
East Anglia Circuit: Cambridge, Suffolk Coast and Royal Norfolk

visits.text: Burnham-on-Crouch earns its nickname: the 'Cowes of the East Coast'. Four sailing and yacht clubs line the River Crouch, and 'Burnham Week'—one of the UK's longest annual regattas—takes place here each September.

Burnham's historic quay lines up its red-roofed houses, old pubs, and still-active boatyards. The Burnham-on-Crouch Museum on the quay is a modest collection on local maritime history and prehistoric habitation on the Dengie peninsula.

activities.text: In summer, cross by ferry to Wallasea Island from Burnham Yacht Harbour. Wallasea is an RSPB marsh nature reserve created from land reclaimed from the sea—a big-wind landscape, rare and little visited.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Kipling wrote when he first saw the house: 'That's She! The Only She! Make an honest woman of her quick!' Bateman's, a Jacobean house from 1634 in Burwash, was his residence from 1902 until his death in 1936. He wrote Puck of Pook's Hill and the poem 'If' here. The dining room with its eighteenth-century silver-leather hangings is the finest room in the house.

The garden at Bateman's, clipped box and mill wheels by the Dudwell: Kipling felt like an English country gentleman here. The thirteenth-century water mill on the property inspired his Puck stories. You can walk to the ponds through the 300-acre estate.

activities.text: Walk around Burwash village before leaving: a bronze statue of Kipling unveiled in 2018 stands in the village centre, piercing gaze. The village preserves seventeenth-century timber-framed façades and a pub, The Bear Inn, that has changed little since the writer's time.

days_plan.itin_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

places.name: Bury St Edmunds

visits.text: The ruins of Bury St Edmunds Abbey stand at the heart of the Abbey Gardens, a public park open daily. This was one of the richest and most powerful Benedictine abbeys in England—founded in 1020 by King Cnut to house the relics of the martyr-king Edmund, killed by Vikings in 869. After Canterbury and Little Walsingham, the abbey was England's third most-visited pilgrimage site. In 1214, the English barons gathered here in secret to swear opposition to King John, an episode that led directly to Magna Carta. Henry VIII dissolved it in 1539 and had it systematically dismantled—historians reckon that if the abbey had survived, Bury St Edmunds would today be larger than Durham or Lincoln. Two remarkable medieval gateways remain: the fourteenth-century Great Gate and the twelfth-century Norman Tower, which still serves as the cathedral's bell tower.

St Edmundsbury Cathedral, right next to the ruins, deserves a detailed visit. Its nave was rebuilt around 1503 by John Wastell—the same architect who completed the fan vaulting of King's College Chapel in Cambridge. The building only gained cathedral status in 1914, when the diocese was created. The Millennium Tower, finished in 2005, at last gives it the profile of a great cathedral. Don't miss Elisabeth Frink's bronze statue of St Edmund, which stands in the precinct.

The archaeological village of West Stow, eight kilometres northwest of Bury St Edmunds, is a full-scale reconstruction of a fifth- to seventh-century Anglo-Saxon village. Excavations from 1965 to 1972 uncovered the foundations of a complete settlement, and houses have since been rebuilt on their original locations using authentic materials and techniques. It's one of the few sites in Europe where you can step inside buildings from that era and understand concretely what daily life looked like 1,500 years ago. The site is surrounded by a fifty-hectare nature reserve on the River Lark.

Make the detour to Ickworth House, seven kilometres southwest of Bury St Edmunds: it's one of England's most eccentric country houses, and it truly deserves the description. The 4th Earl of Bristol began this building in 1796 with a fixed idea: a central rotunda 30 metres high, flanked by two curved wings to display the paintings and sculptures brought back from his travels in Italy. The result looks like nothing else. The property belongs to the National Trust—allow time for the Italianate gardens and the grand landscaped park, which are worth as much as the house itself.

Newmarket, twenty kilometres west of Bury St Edmunds, is the world capital of horse racing. The town has hosted official races since 1622—Charles II himself used to ride there on his own mount. The National Stud, the training yards and Palace House (the former royal residence converted into a horse racing museum) are open to the public. If you pass through on a weekday, you'll see horses training early in the morning on the Heath, a fairly unique experience.

activities.text: Stroll through Bury St Edmunds' historic centre in the evening, between Angel Hill and the Victorian Corn Exchange. The town has an active covered market and a collection of Georgian pubs that make you want to linger. Try the local beers from the Greene King brewery, whose warehouses have been based in the town since 1799.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Road Trip: Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridge
