map_keywords: Castle of Mey Queen Mother Caithness
Ring of Brodgar stone circle Orkney
Old Man of Hoy cliff Orkney

visits.text: Gothic to excess on the façade—turrets, gargoyles, domes—but behind the Victorian stucco, Knebworth House hides a Tudor brick house dating to 1490. This is where Edward Bulwer-Lytton, the novelist who invented the phrase "it was a dark and stormy night," worked in a study adorned with a crystal ball. The Lytton family still lives here. Guided tours available in season.

Knebworth House's park opens onto 250 acres of Hertfordshire countryside, with a dinosaur trail for children, a golden garden, and a rose garden. The view of the façade from the main lawn is the one that appears in all the castle photographs.

activities.text: Get lost in the maze at Knebworth's garden—entry is included in the park ticket. Prefer the house tour in the morning when groups are scarce, and save the maze for the afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Lacock Abbey was founded in 1232 by Ela, Countess of Salisbury, who became the first abbess—and according to several local sources, Wiltshire's first female sheriff. At the dissolution of the monasteries, William Sharington bought the place and converted it into a residence. It was here that William Henry Fox Talbot took the first photographic negative in history in August 1835, at the south gallery window. The Fox Talbot Museum occupies the tithe barn.

activities.text: Green shutters, Tudor doors, facades untouched by modernity: Lacock is almost entirely owned by the National Trust, which explains the absence of satellite dishes and contemporary shopfronts. That's why they filmed Pride and Prejudice, Harry Potter (the Hogwarts classrooms were shot here), Downton Abbey. The George Inn dates from the 14th century and still serves food. Car park £5, free for NT members.

days_plan.itin_title: Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days
London to Bath: Surrey, Hampshire & Wiltshire in 10 Days

visits.text: Prison until 2011, courthouse to this day: Lancaster Castle has been in service for nearly a thousand years. On 20 August 1612, ten people condemned here for witchcraft were hanged on the moor above the town. In the Well Tower, you visit the cells where the Pendle witches were imprisoned. The Crown Court dock still preserves the branding iron used on convicts until 1811. Guided tour compulsory for interior access.

Williamson Park, from the hill, offers the broadest view across Lancaster and Morecambe Bay. The Ashton Memorial, a baroque dome from 1909 financed by a linoleum manufacturer, dominates the park. Too large for a house, too small for a cathedral: Lancastrians themselves aren't quite sure what to make of it. Park entry free. Good 30-minute stop before the return drive to York (approximately 2h45 via A65).

days_plan.itin_title: Gothic Yorkshire: Dracula, Abbeys & Witches – 8 days

places.name: Lavenham

visits.text: Lavenham is England's best-preserved medieval village—more than 340 listed buildings for fewer than 2,000 inhabitants. In the sixteenth century, this tiny settlement paid more tax than York or Lincoln, both ten times larger. Its fortune came from a deep-blue woollen cloth called 'Lavenham Blue', exported as far as Russia and Egypt. When the market collapsed in the early seventeenth century, locals couldn't afford to rebuild in a more modern style—and that's what saved the village. Wander down the High Street looking for The Crooked House: built in 1395 for a wool merchant, this leaning timber-framed house is one of Britain's most photographed, and is sometimes cited as a possible inspiration for the nursery rhyme There Was a Crooked Man—a local association whose actual origins remain uncertain. Lavenham's streets served as the film set for Godric's Hollow in the Harry Potter Deathly Hallows films.

Arriving at Lavenham's Market Place, you can't miss the Guildhall—this timber-framed building from 1530 dominates the square. It was built by the Guild of Corpus Christi to regulate the wool trade, and today it's one of the most accessible gateways to understanding how this town functioned at the height of its wealth. It belongs to the National Trust; allow forty-five minutes for the indoor exhibition on medieval wool trading. Before you leave, look up at the tower of St Peter and St Paul church opposite: 45 metres high, the tallest in Suffolk, commissioned in 1484 by the Spring and de Vere families so the entire region would know they were rich.

Long Melford, eight kilometres south of Lavenham, is another wool town whose High Street stretches for more than three kilometres—one of the longest in England. Stop at Holy Trinity church: with its 45 great windows of fifteenth-century stained glass, it's one of Suffolk's finest Perpendicular churches, and the atmosphere inside is particularly luminous. Next door, Melford Hall, a Tudor mansion from the 1560s, welcomes the public under the National Trust. The novelist Beatrix Potter regularly stayed here with her cousins.

If you pass through Sudbury, step into Gainsborough's House—it's the birthplace of Thomas Gainsborough (1727–1788), one of the greatest English portrait painters of the eighteenth century, and the only museum in Britain dedicated to a major artist housed in his birthplace. You'll find the world's largest permanent collection of his works. It's a short, pleasant visit with no queue. Coming out, look for the bronze statue of Gainsborough in the market square, palette in hand—he appears to be watching over the town he spent his life trying to escape for London.

activities.text: Wander Lavenham's lanes and hidden courtyards in late afternoon when the tour coaches have left. The village is alive—bakery, galleries, antique shops, tea rooms—and it's a pleasure to explore on foot. Several waymarked circuits of 2–4 kilometres start from the car park opposite the church and cross the surrounding fields.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Road Trip: Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridge
From Cambridge to London: Eastern England in 5 Days
East Anglia, Cambridge & Rutland: 13-Day Road Trip

map_keywords: St Peter and St Paul Church Lavenham
The Crooked House Lavenham High Street

visits.text: Runswick Bay is perhaps the most beautiful coastal village on England's entire north-east coast—regularly ranked among Britain's best beaches by the Sunday Times. Red-tiled cottages huddle against the cliff face, linked by staircases so steep the road descends at a gradient of 1 in 4. What the postcards don't tell you: in 1682 (not 1664 as some sources claim), the entire village slid into the sea during a winter's night. The only thing that saved everyone: villagers were keeping vigil over a corpse and managed to wake the others in time. Coins and a silver spoon were still being found on the beach in 1817. The village was rebuilt a little further south, and the houses still cling to the same unstable clays.

Lealholm, in the Esk Valley 35 minutes inland by car, is the perfect antidote after the coast. This stone village threaded by the Esk walks itself in 20 minutes: 18th-century humpback bridge, stepping stones in the riverbed, overflowing gardens above the water. The atmosphere is that of deep Yorkshire unchanged since the late 19th century. The Board Inn, a riverside pub, serves dinner.

activities.text: The morning at Runswick Bay is ideally spent on the beach. At low tide, the exposed Jurassic shale rocks at Hob Holes—the caves that locals believed were inhabited by a benevolent elf capable of curing children's whooping cough—reveal fossil ammonites and bivalves. The Cleveland Way skirts the bay: an hour's walk toward Staithes to the north or Sandsend to the south is enough to find yourself alone facing the sea.

In the afternoon, climb up the Esk Valley to Lealholm. The road winding from the coast inland crosses open moorland before plunging into the wooded valley—the contrast is striking. In Lealholm, cross the Esk on the stepping stones, walk along the cottage gardens by the water, and end the day riverside.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands

visits.text: The Royal Armouries Museum is Leeds' big surprise - free, by the Leeds Dock, and frankly impressive. What was crammed into the Tower of London for lack of space was transferred here in 1996 and fills five floors. Start with the Hall of Steel: 2,500 pieces of military equipment arranged over the building's full height like an immense tapestry of arms. Then find the 16th-century Indian elephant armour on the fourth floor - the only one known in a public museum worldwide, mail and plates for an entire animal. And Henry VIII's tournament armours: they prove he was, when young, slim and athletic. Late portraits show only half the story. Allow a good half-day.

The Victoria Quarter in Leeds centre is the best lesson in Victorian architecture you'll find in a shopping centre. Architect Frank Matcham - better known for his theatres - designed in 1900 arcades in marble, mahogany and rose terracotta, with a canopy that's Europe's largest in coloured glass. The Art Nouveau golden mosaics under the central dome represent the trades that made Leeds' fortune. Harvey Nichols opened its first shop outside London here in 1996. You can enter just to look at the ceiling.

If you have a car and 30 minutes, detour via Kirkstall Abbey - 5km west of the centre along the River Aire. Founded in 1152 by Cistercian monks from Fountains Abbey, dissolved by Henry VIII in 1539, its ruins are among England's best preserved: in some parts, walls stand to original roof level. Turner and Girtin painted these ruins when Romanticism had transformed old stones into artistic subjects. It's a public park, free entry, quiet, by the water.

activities.text: Leeds Corn Exchange - a grain exchange built in 1863 - is a masterpiece of cast iron and glass with an elliptical dome reminiscent of Paris's Bourse de Commerce. Converted into a market of independent shops: record stores, local designers, cafés. Right next door, Leeds Kirkgate Market has been active since the Middle Ages and remains one of Europe's largest covered markets - hundreds of stalls, real neighbourhood life.

Leeds is 2h15 from London King's Cross by direct train - one of northern England's best-served routes. The station is a 20-minute walk from the Dock. If you're continuing elsewhere, Birmingham, Manchester and Edinburgh are all accessible in under 2h30 from here.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake District and North-East Loop: Lakes, Dales and Cathedrals

visits.text: The castle floats. Set on two islands in a natural lake amid 500 acres of Kent parkland, Leeds Castle is one of England's most photographed medieval sites. The first stone fortification dates to 1119; Edward I turned it into a royal palace in 1278 for Queen Eleanor of Castile. Six queens of England lived here. Lady Baillie, the last private owner, bequeathed everything to a foundation on her death in 1974.

You'd expect an armory, you find a dog collar museum in the old stables. The collection—the world's largest of its type—assembles over 130 pieces from the 16th to the 19th century, donated to the foundation in 1977 by Gertrude Hunt. The earliest iron-spiked examples protected hunting dogs' throats against wolves and bears that ran through England's forests. Included in the ticket.

A dog collar museum in a medieval castle, and also: a birds of prey aviary, black swans on the lake, daily falconry, the Gloriette—a 13th-century keep with reconstructed royal apartments. Henry VIII undertook major works here in the 16th century. The multimedia audio guide included in the ticket covers nine centuries of history.

activities.text: Get lost in the maze of 2,400 yews planted in 1988—designed to truly lose you: the classic 'hand on the wall' rule doesn't work here, the walls aren't all connected. At the center, an underground grotto with mythological deities carved into the walls. Allow 30 to 45 minutes.

Attend Leeds Castle's daily falconry—buzzards, hawks, and eagles in free flight over the park with commentary from the falconers. The estate also houses an exotic bird aviary and the famous black swans on the lake. Displays usually run twice daily depending on season; times posted at the entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Surrey to Kent: Castles and Gardens in 10 Days

places.name: Leicester

visits.text: In August 2012, archaeologists from the University of Leicester began digging in a city centre car park. On the first day, they unearthed human bones. On 4 February 2013, the university announced 'beyond reasonable doubt' that they'd found the remains of Richard III, last Plantagenet king, killed at Bosworth in 1485. The King Richard III Visitor Centre, built on the exact site of the car park and grave, tells the story of his life, death and 527 years of oblivion. His tomb is at Leicester Cathedral, less than 100 metres away—reinterred in 2015 during a televised ceremony in the presence of the Archbishop of Canterbury.

The Jewry Wall, steps from the centre, is one of the largest sections of Roman wall still standing in England—9 metres high, dating from the 2nd century, remains of the public baths of the Roman city of Ratae Corieltauvorum. Don't miss the adjacent museum: the Roman mosaics displayed in situ in their original context are far more eloquent than a standard museum case, and entry is free. A brief stop but one that completely changes how you see the city.

The National Space Centre is one of the UK's most impressive science museums—entry includes a session in the country's largest planetarium. The 42-metre tower houses rockets in vertical position. The University of Leicester coordinated the mission control for Beagle 2, the British Mars probe launched in 2003—the first ESA mission with public access to the control centre. Allow at least 2 hours.

Leicester's outdoor market is Europe's largest covered and open-air market, active since the 12th century. The stalls reflect the city's diversity. The Belgrave Road district, nicknamed 'the Golden Mile', lines up South Asian restaurants and shops for 1.5km—some of England's finest curries outside Birmingham or London are found here.

activities.text: Twenty-five kilometres southwest of Leicester, the site of the Battle of Bosworth (22 August 1485) is where Richard III met his death in the last major clash of the Wars of the Roses. The Bosworth Battlefield Heritage Centre offers a reconstruction of the terrain and an exhibition on the discovery of the king's remains in 2012. Medieval re-enactments take place in August.

days_plan.itin_title: The Midlands and Peak District Essentials

map_keywords: King Richard III Visitor Centre Leicester

visits.text: The floor at Cockle Shed Row in Old Leigh has crunched underfoot for centuries: cockles have been fished in the Thames estuary and sold in tubs here since the 19th century, and the family fishing boats moored just opposite haven't fundamentally changed.

The Leigh Heritage Centre occupies a former forge on the old town's High Street. Photographic exhibitions and fishermen's objects trace the life of this port that declined in the 19th century when the railway line to Southend diverted trade. Free entry.

activities.text: Cockles at Osborne Brothers cost a few pounds a pint—and you can eat on the terrace facing the Thames estuary, in cafés or quayside. It's one of Essex's most authentic seafood addresses.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Lincoln Cathedral was, for more than two centuries, the tallest building in the world. Built from 1072 on William the Conqueror's orders, a spire added in 1311 exceeded Egypt's pyramids—until it collapsed in a storm in 1549. Today the central tower peaks at 81 metres. The interior is one of England's great Gothic cathedrals: seek out the Lincoln Imp, a small sculpture of a grimacing demon in the Angel Choir—legend says he came to wreak havoc before being turned to stone by an angel. Book the Roof Top Tour ahead for panoramic views from the summit.

Lincoln Castle, built by William the Conqueror in 1068, houses one of only four surviving copies of the 1215 Magna Carta. It's functioned as a courthouse for 900 years. The Wall Walk lets you circle the complete ramparts with views of the cathedral on one side and the Lincolnshire countryside on the other. The old Victorian prison, with its system of total isolation between prisoners, is one of England's most unsettling.

Steep Hill is one of England's finest shopping streets. Independent shops, antique dealers, bookshops and cafés occupy 14th- and 15th-century timber-framed houses. At the top, the Jew's House is one of England's oldest inhabited houses still standing, dating from 1150. At the bottom, High Bridge is England's oldest bridge still in use to have houses built on it—12th century.

Newport Arch is a Roman gate from the 2nd century still standing—the only one in Britain still used by motor traffic. The Romans founded Lincoln (Lindum Colonia) around 48 AD as a legionary fortress. Around it, Bailgate's streets preserve the original Roman orthogonal plan. The Medieval Bishop's Palace, just behind the cathedral, is one of England's most important medieval residences—its vineyard, a gift from the twin town of Neustadt in Germany, is still in production.

Brayford Waterfront, at the foot of the hill, is one of England's oldest natural harbours—of Roman origin. Today it's Lincoln's lively quarter of bars and restaurants, with the university campus as backdrop. It's the ideal place to end the road trip: a waterside terrace, the cathedral silhouetted on the hill above.

activities.text: The International Bomber Command Centre, 3km from the centre, is one of the most moving Second World War memorials in the UK. Bomber Command suffered the heaviest losses of all British units—55,573 men killed. The 31-metre central spire corresponds to a Lancaster's wingspan. The walls bear the 57,861 names of the dead. Lincolnshire was nicknamed 'Bomber County' during the war: more RAF bases than any other English county. Free entry.

days_plan.itin_title: The Midlands and Peak District Essentials
England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes

visits.text: The first Viking raid in documented history took place here on 8 June 793 - a shock for all Western Christendom. Lindisfarne monastery had been founded in 635 by Saint Aidan. The Lindisfarne Gospels were created here, around 710-725, a masterpiece of Insular art preserved in the British Library. The visible ruins are those of the 12th-century Benedictine priory.

Lindisfarne's Tudor castle was transformed in 1902 by architect Edwin Lutyens for publisher Edward Hudson, in an Arts & Crafts interior style contrasting with the exterior fortress. The garden was designed by Gertrude Jekyll. The view from the ramparts over the bay and Bamburgh Castle is one of Northumberland's finest.

activities.text: Check the tides: the 1.3 km causeway disappears twice daily for several hours. The crossing is passable roughly 3 hours after high tide until 2 hours before the next. Cars get caught there regularly every year - shame and towing included. Tide tables are posted at the causeway entrance and on Northumberland's official website.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

visits.text: Start your day at Albert Dock – its red-brick warehouses and cast-iron columns beside the Mersey rank among the 19th century's industrial finest. Opened in 1846, it was the first dock warehouse built entirely without timber beams, to prevent cargo fires. Now UNESCO-listed, the complex houses Tate Liverpool, the Merseyside Maritime Museum, and The Beatles Story museum – you'll have plenty to occupy your day.

The Beatles Story, in Albert Dock, is the world's largest museum dedicated to the group. It traces their journey with recreations of the Cavern Club, Abbey Road studios, and their Hamburg apartment. What makes it special: the audioguide is narrated by Julia Baird, John Lennon's sister – a voice that changes your relationship to the anecdotes. Allow 2 hours for the complete circuit.

Push open the Cavern Club's door at 10 Mathew Street – here the Beatles played 292 times between 9 February 1961 and 3 August 1963, and Brian Epstein discovered them from the audience. The original was demolished in 1973; today's Cavern was reconstructed in 1984 with 15,000 bricks salvaged from 75% of the original location. Even if you're not a particular Beatles fan, enter: the venue hosts live music every afternoon, and the vaulted brick cellar atmosphere has something unique.

activities.text: The Magical Mystery Tour explores key sites from the Beatles' childhood: John Lennon's childhood home in Woolton, Paul McCartney's house, Strawberry Field, and Penny Lane (the real places). The circuit lasts around 2 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes

visits.text: The climb isn't easy, but at the summit of Yr Wyddfa at 1,085 metres you'll stand at the highest point in Wales and England—with Ireland and the Isle of Man sometimes visible on clear days. Six different paths lead to the top; the most accessible is the Llanberis Path from the village. Count on 6 to 8 hours round-trip on foot. Weather changes brutally: set off with waterproofs even in fine weather.

activities.text: Opened in 1896, this rack-and-pinion railway is the only one of its kind still operating in the UK, climbing 7.6 km from Llanberis to the summit. For those who don't fancy walking 6 hours—or who want to descend without shattered knees—it solves the problem elegantly. Hafod Eryri cafe at the summit is open when the train runs. Book online: places sell out fast in July-August.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

visits.text: Discover Llandudno, Wales' largest seaside resort, a beautifully preserved Victorian town nestled between two limestone headlands on the North Wales coast. The elegant sweep of the promenade, lined with pastel-painted hotels and guest houses, curves around the bay with the Great Orme rising dramatically at its northern end. This classic British seaside town has maintained its Victorian character while offering modern attractions.

Ride the Great Orme Tramway, Britain's only cable-hauled street tramway, which has carried passengers up the headland since 1902. The vintage trams climb through residential streets before emerging onto the open hillside with spectacular views unfolding across the bay, town and mountains beyond. At the summit, panoramic vistas stretch across the Irish Sea to the Isle of Man on clear days.

Take the Llandudno Cable Cars for a different perspective on the Great Orme's dramatic landscapes and the town spread out below. The longest passenger cable car system in Britain glides over limestone grasslands grazed by wild Kashmir goats, descendants of a herd gifted by the Shah of Persia in 1837. The summit offers walking trails, an ancient copper mine dating back 4,000 years, and endless views over sea and shore.

activities.text: Walk or drive to the summit of the Great Orme for sunset views over the Irish Sea and Snowdonia mountains

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia

visits.text: A canal suspended 38 metres above the Dee valley, with no guardrails on the water side, and boats still crossing it today. Pontcysyllte is Britain's highest and longest navigable aqueduct, designed by Thomas Telford and completed in 1805, inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2009. The 18 hollow masonry pillars support a cast-iron trough whose mortar was, according to local tradition, strengthened with lime and ox blood. Walk the towpath: it's free and the vertigo is guaranteed.

activities.text: Board one of the motorboats departing from Trevor's Canal Wharf, opposite the aqueduct. The 45-minute return crossing offers a perspective the towpath walk cannot: finding yourself suspended above the void, level with the crows. Departures at regular intervals in season; no reservations needed for small groups.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

places.name: Lochboisdale

visits.text: The causeway from North Uist to Benbecula and on to South Uist is one of the great drives of the Outer Hebrides - 40 km of connected islands linked by causeways built in the 1940s, threading through a watery landscape of tidal flats and sky. Benbecula is the flattest of all the islands, its highest hill only 124 metres; the central moorland is a stronghold for breeding hen harriers and short-eared owls. Cross through without stopping if you must, but pull over at Stinky Bay on Benbecula (named for the kelp that washes ashore after Atlantic storms) and scan the shallow water - grey and common seals haul out here.

South Uist's west coast is 20 miles of continuous white shell sand beach backed by machair - the richest coastal grassland habitat in Europe, explosively colourful in June and July with wildflowers that include rare orchids, bog pimpernel and Irish lady's tresses. The east side is mountainous and rugged. The contrast between the two coasts - you can see both from almost any high point - gives South Uist its distinct character. At Howmore, a small cluster of traditional thatched blackhouses survives in a working crofting township; the Hebridean Trust maintains them and they can be visited. Near Loch Druidibeg in the centre, a small nature reserve hosts one of the few surviving wild Greylag geese breeding populations in Scotland.

The causeway to Eriskay drops you onto a tiny island - 170 people, a single-track road - that carries more historical weight than most. This is where Bonnie Prince Charlie first set foot on Scottish soil in July 1745, landing on a small beach now called Coileag a' Phrionnsa (the Prince's Beach). In 1941, the SS Politician ran aground here carrying 260,000 bottles of whisky bound for the United States. Local islanders, facing wartime shortages, helped themselves to an estimated 24,000 bottles - some paddling out in darkness, some disguised in their wives' clothing to avoid oil stains. The Customs officer who tried to prosecute them became one of the least popular men in the Hebrides. Compton Mackenzie turned the whole episode into the novel Whisky Galore in 1947. Head to Am Politician pub to see salvaged bottles from the wreck.

activities.text: The beach at Coileag a' Phrionnsa on Eriskay is a short walk from the causeway end and worth 20 minutes of your evening. Pink convolvulus - known nowhere else in Scotland except on this beach, where it was supposedly left by the Prince - still blooms here in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain

map_keywords: SS Politician Eriskay Am Politician pub

visits.text: North of Ullapool, the road enters Assynt - a landscape unlike anything south of it. Here the mountains don't form ranges but stand alone, isolated on a flat moorland plain like pins in a map: Stac Pollaidh (613 m), Cùl Mòr (849 m), Suilven (731 m). Suilven is the most dramatic, a long whale-backed ridge that appears from the south as a single improbable sugarloaf. The first geological survey of this area in the 19th century triggered one of the most important revolutions in the history of geology: two local quarrymen - not professional scientists - correctly identified that the Moine Thrust Sheet had pushed ancient rock over younger rock, overturning the assumption that older rock always lay beneath younger. Archibald Geikie of the Geological Survey initially dismissed their findings, then was proved wrong.

The ruined Ardvreck Castle, jutting from a promontory into Loch Assynt, is one of the most photogenic ruins on the NC500 - three storeys of 15th-century MacLeod tower reflected in still water. It is free to visit and the walk from the parking area takes 5 minutes. James Graham, 1st Marquess of Montrose, was held prisoner here in 1650 after his defeat at the Battle of Carbisdale, before being handed to the government and executed in Edinburgh.

Lochinver is a real working fishing harbour - langoustines, haddock, monkfish - and the best place to eat on this section of the route. The Lochinver Larder, a bakery attached to a small restaurant, makes pies that have achieved cult status on the NC500: venison and cranberry, smoked haddock and cheddar, haggis, neeps and tatties. Arrive before noon if you want the best selection.

activities.text: The ascent of Stac Pollaidh from the car park on the B869 is one of the best short hillwalks in Sutherland: 3 km return, 350 m of ascent, with scrambling near the top and a spectacular ridge view over the Assynt lochs and sea. Allow 3 hours including the scramble. The path is well marked. For those who don't want to scramble to the summit, the walk to the bealach (col) at the base of the pinnacles is itself worth 2 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Scottish Highlands Road Trip: Castles, Lochs and Wild Landscapes
North Coast 500 with Orkney: Scotland's Ultimate Coastal Circuit

places.name: Lochmaddy

visits.text: The ferry from Leverburgh to Berneray takes about an hour, winding between the skerries of the Sound of Harris - one of the most navigationally complex stretches of water in Scotland, and all the better for it. Berneray itself is a small island of about 140 people connected to North Uist by causeway, and its west beach, Tràigh Iar, is a three-mile stretch of clean white shell sand almost always deserted. Prince Charles camped here incognito in 1987 and worked with a local crofting family - a fact that still raises an affectionate eyebrow among locals.

North Uist is a different kind of landscape from Harris: low-lying, extraordinarily watery, more than half the surface covered by lochs, lochans and bog. The A865 runs a loop around the island and passes the RSPB Balranald reserve, one of the best sites in Britain for the corncrake - a secretive moorland bird whose rasping call you'll hear before you see it, if you hear it at all. In summer the machair behind the beaches here is a carpet of wildflowers: clover, yellow rattle, orchids, eyebright. Barpa Langais, a chambered Neolithic cairn visible from the road, is one of the best-preserved passage tombs in the Outer Hebrides, dating to around 3500 BC.

activities.text: The Taigh Chearsabhagh Museum and Arts Centre in Lochmaddy is the cultural hub of the Uists - a small but beautifully curated collection covering the natural and human history of the island group, with rotating art exhibitions. It also runs wildlife boat trips into the sea lochs of eastern North Uist, which are excellent otter territory. The café serves good local produce.

White-tailed eagles - locally known as 'flying barn doors' for their 2.5-metre wingspan - were reintroduced to the Outer Hebrides and are now regularly seen over North Uist and Benbecula. Golden eagles are also present. For the best chances, scan the hillsides east of the A867 near Loch Euphort in the early morning or late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain

map_keywords: Barpa Langais chambered cairn North Uist

places.name: London

visits.text: Ten heads fell here, within the privacy of Tower Green—high-ranking ladies were granted the private scaffold rather than the crowds at Tower Hill. Anne Boleyn, beheaded by sword on 19 May 1536, lies buried in the Chapel of St Peter ad Vincula, thirty metres away. Henry VIII had sent for an executioner from Calais: a final favour. The White Tower dates from 1078; allow 2 to 3 hours, avoid July weekends.

Beneath the paving stones of Trinity Square, grass grows neatly over roughly 125 engraved names. First death in 1381, last in 1747—Simon Fraser, Lord Lovat, beheaded on 9 April 1747, the last man to suffer this honour publicly in England. That day, a spectator stand collapsed and killed several people. The memorial is discreet, tucked to the left of the large war monument. Easy to miss: look for the low chains on the ground.

The oldest church in the City, yet almost nobody stops here. Founded in 675 by Barking Abbey, it predates the Tower of London by more than 400 years. In the crypt: Roman pavement from the 2nd century, discovered in 1926, and the temporary remains of Thomas More and Archbishop Laud, laid here after their beheadings on Tower Hill. Free entry, crypt open most days.

Nothing to see, in theory. An ordinary courtyard wedged between buildings. Yet at 1:45am on 30 September 1888, Constable Watkins shone his lantern into the south-west corner and recoiled in horror: the mutilated body of Catherine Eddowes. The only Ripper crime committed within the City of London, outside Met jurisdiction. The old cobbled yard was transformed into a garden around 2013—the original dark corner has vanished.

Number 29 no longer exists. Locals just call it 'the brewery wall'. At 5:30am on 8 September 1888, Annie Chapman was talking with a man outside this doorway. Thirty minutes later, her mutilated body was discovered in the back yard by John Davis, a tenant, on his way to the toilet. Her uterus had been taken. Today: street art, curry houses, Sunday brunch at the market. The 136 years weigh lightly.

The smell of wet ivy and cold stone hits you at the entrance. The West Cemetery (1839) can only be visited on guided tours: Egyptian Avenue, Lebanon Circle—catacombs overgrown by an ancient cedar. Allow 1.5 hours for the guided visit. Booking mandatory via the Friends of Highgate Cemetery Trust website, places limited, often fully booked two weeks ahead in autumn.

Marx's bust has been bombed twice, in 1965 and 1970. Replaced, it now sits atop a massive plinth. Douglas Adams's grave is covered with pens left by fans. George Eliot's, more discreet, is two rows over. The East side is open access for a few quid; arrive at opening (10am) to avoid the groups.

In 1822, a surgical theatre was slipped into the attic of a Southwark church, level with the women's ward of St Thomas's Hospital. Tiered like a proper theatre, it hosted medical students while destitute women were amputated without anaesthesia or antiseptic. Rediscovered in 1956 behind a bricked-up skylight, opened as a museum in 1962. Access via a 52-step spiral staircase: be warned.

The Bishop of Winchester collected taxes from them, but the Church refused them Christian burial. Medieval Southwark prostitutes—the 'Winchester Geese'—were buried in this unconsecrated ground from the late Middle Ages. In 1992, archaeologists exhumed 148 bodies during Jubilee Line works. More than a third were infants. Today a memorial garden, red gates covered with ribbons. Redcross Way, SE1.

A museum about health, founded by a man who sold medicines across the entire world. Henry Wellcome (1853–1936) amassed more than a million objects. The permanent gallery 'Medicine Man' displays death masks, medieval surgical instruments, and a chastity belt side by side. Free entry, Euston Road. Perfect in the morning, before the school groups arrive.

Three thousand years of embalming arranged in the dim light of rooms 62–63 at the British Museum. The mummy collection is the largest outside Cairo, according to the museum's Egypt and Sudan department. Then move to room 50: Lindow Man, discovered in 1984 in a Cheshire peat bog, is so well preserved you can still make out his beard, his skin, and the rope with which he was killed. Free entry, online booking advised.

Descend into the crypt on the first Tuesday of the month, from 6pm to 9pm, when all the lights go out and the candles come on. The sarcophagus of Seti I—bought by John Soane in 1824 for £2,000 when the British Museum refused to pay the price—is carved from translucent alabaster, covered with hieroglyphs from the Book of Gates. The British Museum had declined the same offer from explorer Belzoni. Very popular events: book several weeks ahead.

activities.text: Track down authentic London fish and chips: find a traditional chippy in the lanes around Borough Market and order a portion with malt vinegar—that's how locals have it. The batter should be light and crispy, the fish white and flaky. A full meal for under ten quid.

Afternoon tea became an institution thanks to the Duchess of Bedford in 1840, who made a habit of filling the 5pm gap with tea and small cakes. Book one of the grand historic hotels in the centre for the ceremonial version: warm scones, cucumber-cream cheese sandwiches, cakes and tea cakes served on a tiered stand. Advance booking essential, especially on weekdays.

London's gin bars are one of the capital's great pleasures: the city has several dozen working distilleries, and specialist bars offer lists running into the hundreds, with tonics and garnishes matched to each gin. It's a lovely way to close a circuit that has crossed several gin terroirs—Devon, Bristol, Cambridge—and compare the styles.

For a farewell dinner worthy of the trip, several London restaurants serve British haute cuisine that rivals the great French tables. Rules has been an institution since 1798. Berners Tavern in the London Edition hotel, or St John in Farringdon—celebrated over the years for its nose-to-tail cooking that influenced an entire generation of chefs—are excellent choices depending on your budget. Book ahead.

Set your pint down on the 1845 tiled bar and let the blue and white azulejos tell the story of old Spitalfields. The Ten Bells has stood here since at least 1753; Mary Kelly, the Ripper's last canonical victim, drank here on the evening of 8 November 1888, before being killed that night. Between 1976 and 1988, the pub was called 'The Jack the Ripper'. A feminist campaign secured the return to the original name. Packed on Friday nights; go midweek.

If you take the tour on a Friday evening, the lanes are half-empty and the guides speak candidly. Donald Rumbelow and Richard Jones are among the most serious historians of the subject—their tours leave from Aldgate East. Duration roughly 2 hours. Book ahead in October, places sell out fast.

In March 1970, hundreds of Londoners armed with stakes and crucifixes stormed the closed cemetery after a television report on ITV broadcast on a Friday the 13th. Two rival vampire hunters—David Farrant and Sean Manchester—had publicly disputed exclusive tracking rights. Farrant was later arrested in the adjacent cemetery, equipped with a stake and crucifix. No vampire was ever found. The Flask pub in Highgate Village, frequented since the 18th century, makes a fine end-of-day stop.

Track down authentic London fish and chips: find a traditional chippy in the lanes around Borough Market and order a portion with malt vinegar—that's how locals have it. The batter should be light and crispy, the fish white and flaky. A full meal for under ten quid.

map_keywords: All Hallows by the Tower London

visits.text: The London Eye was inaugurated on 31 December 1999 by Tony Blair - but didn't admit its first paying passenger until 9 March 2000. The structure stands 135 metres tall, with 32 air-conditioned capsules (one for each London borough) that can hold 25 people each. The cabins are numbered 1 to 33 - number 13 is deliberately omitted. A complete rotation takes 30 minutes at 0.9 km/h: the wheel doesn't stop, you board while it's moving. From the top, the view extends 40km on a clear day. Book online to skip the queue. For the best light, go an hour before sunset.

activities.text: The South Bank, along the south bank of the Thames from the London Eye to Tate Modern, is one of London's liveliest pedestrian routes. You'll pass the BFI Southbank, the National Theatre (opened 1976), and under the arches of Hungerford Bridge where street performers play constantly. Count on 45 minutes to an hour if you really stroll.

In the evening, the London Eye lights up with over 5,500 LEDs - the colours change according to events. The view is most spectacular from Westminster Bridge or from the north bank. If you're there on a weekday evening off-season, crowds are noticeably lighter than weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: The London Museum (formerly Museum of London) is relocating from its London Wall site, which it has occupied since 1976, to Smithfield Market where it's due to reopen in late 2026 in a building renovated for £337 million. Meanwhile, the Museum of London Docklands in East London remains open and offers exhibitions on the history of the port, river and London's trade. When the new museum opens at Smithfield, it will present over 6 million objects telling the story of London across 6,000 years—from Roman Londinium to the 2000s. Check the opening progress before planning your visit.

activities.text: While awaiting the opening of the new Smithfield site, the Museum of London Docklands in East London remains an excellent alternative for the city's port and commercial history.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: Kentwell Hall hides behind an avenue of centuries-old oaks—the surprise is total when the Tudor manor appears with its moat, 30 acres of gardens, and rare-breed farm. Built in the sixteenth century for the Clopton family, enriched by the wool trade, Kentwell is famous for its Tudor historical re-enactments organised since 1979. Open April to October, Wednesday to Sunday.

Melford Hall looks austere from outside—red bricks, octagonal turrets, dry moat—but the interior reveals a collection of Dutch marines and a Chinese bedroom. This National Trust property dates from the sixteenth century and was regularly visited by Beatrix Potter. Its terraced garden on Long Melford's great lawn is worth the visit alone.

activities.text: You walk the impressive Long Melford green—two kilometres of meadow bordered by Georgian houses and red-brick pubs. It's one of the longest village greens in England. At the northern end stands Holy Trinity Church, whose Flemish stained glass and carved porch make it Suffolk's most-visited church after Lavenham's.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia, Cambridge & Rutland: 13-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Climb up to Ludlow Castle and look for a discreet panel about Prince Arthur - eldest son of Henry VII, first husband of Catherine of Aragon, who died here of tuberculosis in 1502 at 15, just months after his wedding. His premature death allowed his younger brother to inherit the throne and become Henry VIII. The castle had been the administrative capital of Wales and the Marches since 1472, and remained royal property until 1811. The ruins are well preserved, and the view from the towers over the Teme valley makes the climb worthwhile.

Walk up Broad Street at a leisurely pace - poet John Betjeman called Ludlow "probably the loveliest town in England," and this street proves his enthusiasm justified. Five hundred listed buildings, an improbable mix of Norman, Tudor, Georgian, and Victorian facades that coexist without crowding each other. The Buttercross at the top of the street marks the location of the old medieval butter market.

St Laurence's Church deserves 30 minutes. It's one of the largest parish churches in Shropshire, in English Perpendicular style, with a remarkable carved rood screen. The ashes of poet A.E. Housman, author of A Shropshire Lad, were scattered here at his request. The tower offers a viewpoint over the entire town.

Drive 12 km north and stop at Stokesay Castle - one of the best-preserved medieval fortified manor houses in England. Built around 1291 by a wool merchant named Laurence de Ludlow who had made his fortune supplying the crown, it has undergone no major modifications since the 17th century. English Heritage manages the site: you can enter the halls as they were in the Middle Ages, with original floorboards, fireplaces, and dovecote. Allow 1h30 on site.

Continue to Church Stretton, nicknamed "Little Switzerland" by the Victorians who came here to recuperate - the comparison is flattering but the setting is indeed spectacular. The village sits between the hills of the Long Mynd to the west and Caer Caradoc to the east. If the weather is clear, the view from the Long Mynd ridges reaches Wales.

activities.text: Ludlow is Shropshire's gastronomic capital - the town has had up to three Michelin-starred restaurants for 10,000 inhabitants, making it the most starred per capita in England. What's more striking in practice is that even the grocers and pubs play the local card: menus display the names of local producers, sausages, cheeses, cider from neighboring Herefordshire. In September, the Ludlow Food and Drink Festival takes over the castle courtyard - if you're there that weekend, book well in advance.

Whitcliffe Common, a 10-minute walk from the castle, offers the best panorama of the town: red tile roofs, church spires, castle ruins on their spur above the Teme. Go in the late afternoon for the light. This is where locals walk their dogs - an unpretentious place, and all the more pleasant for it.

Cardingmill Valley, a National Trust property 5 minutes from Church Stretton, is the ideal starting point for a hike on the Shropshire hills. A path follows the stream up to the moorlands of the Long Mynd plateau - allow 2h round trip for the panorama from the summit, with no major technical difficulty. The National Trust cafe at the bottom of the valley is perfect for recovering afterwards.

Back in Ludlow in the evening, take the opportunity to dine properly. The town deserves a serious table - good addresses abound on Church Street and Broad Street, and the kitchens all rely on local producers from Shropshire and Herefordshire. Book if you're coming on a weekend.

days_plan.itin_title: England's Gastronomic Trail: Markets, Pubs & Culinary Heritage
England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes
Midlands and Peak District Road Trip: Heritage and Wild Landscapes
Midlands and Peak District Road Trip: Heritage and Wild Landscapes
Welsh Marches, Cotswolds and Bath in 11 days

visits.text: Lulworth Cove looks like someone punched the coast. Almost-circular cove, carved roughly 10,000 years ago when the sea breached the Portland limestone and eroded the softer layers behind. The visitor centre explains everything (free), then climb to Stair Hole to see the Lulworth Crumple—spectacular rock folds frozen in stone.

The cove forms part of the Jurassic Coast, England's first natural UNESCO site inscribed in 2001. Ninety-five miles from Exmouth to Studland, exposing 185 million years of rock. Water's surprisingly clear and sheltered in summer for a quick dip.

activities.text: Parking costs £5-10 depending on duration near the visitor centre. Avoid August weekends—it overflows. The Boat Shed Café overlooks the cove for a breather. Reckon 30 minutes' walk via the coast path to Durdle Door if you're skipping the car.

days_plan.itin_title: England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton

visits.text: Britain's largest freshwater lake deserves a pause: 71 km² of surface area, 190 m deep, 21 islands. Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park, created in 2002, covers 1,865 km². Ben Lomond, visible from the shore, peaks at 974 m and can be climbed in 4 hours return from Rowardennan.

Luss residents filmed the TV series Take the High Road here for years, turning the village into an attraction in its own right. The pink schist cottages, manicured gardens, and direct loch views make it one of the park's finest showcases. The village kirk dates from the 13th century.

activities.text: If the weather holds, take a boat from Luss to see the loch from the water. Several operators run 30- to 60-minute cruises. On foot, the West Highland Way hugs the east shore—even two hours' walk from Balmaha gives you a sense of the landscape's scale.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland: Highlands & Skye in 11 days

visits.text: Madame Tussauds is an attraction founded in London in 1835 by Marie Tussaud, whose origins trace back to the French Revolution—she had created the death masks of Marie Antoinette and Louis XVI. The museum now houses hundreds of hyper-realistic wax figures of global personalities arranged in themed settings. If you're visiting with children or celebrity enthusiasts, book your time slot in advance as queues get long during peak season.

activities.text: Book tickets online in advance—on-site prices are significantly higher and queues can exceed an hour in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: The red-brick sails of Thames barges dominate the Hythe quay at Maldon—among the few dozen rigged specimens still in service from the 2,000 registered at the turn of the 20th century. Barges like the Thistle (1895) and Hydrogen (1906) offer sea trips from the quay.

Maldon's Moot Hall dates to the 15th century: it served as both town council hall and courthouse. Still used by the town hall, it's accessible on certain days in season. The stepped brick tower is one of Essex's finest medieval façades.

The 991 Battle of Maldon—a Viking victory over the Saxons—is commemorated by a statue of Byrhtnoth on the waterfront and by an Anglo-Saxon poem that appears in all English literary history textbooks. The battle scene took place on the Blackwater Estuary bridge, visible from the quay.

activities.text: Board a Thames barge from Maldon's Hythe Quay with the Thames Sailing Barge Trust or Topsail Charters. Trips range from 2 hours (estuary cruise) to full day. Advance booking required.

Taste Blackwater Estuary oysters and Maldon salt—salt production in this area dates to the Domesday Book (1086), and the Maldon Salt company has made its sea salt flakes since 1882, now used in restaurants worldwide. The factory shop opens weekdays.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: The road from Haworth crosses the Dales from the north—allow time for the approach. Stop at Ribblehead for the viaduct carrying the Settle-Carlisle line across the moor since 1874. The figures are dizzying: 24 arches, 404 meters long, 32 meters above ground, 1.5 million bricks, and over 100 workers dead during the 5-year construction—the Midland Railway funded expansion of the local cemetery to bury them. Every sixth pier is 50% thicker than the others, designed to maintain the structure if a pier were to collapse. In 1989, British Rail nearly closed the line before citizen mobilization saved it. Passenger trains still run today—if you see one pass, the spectacle is breathtaking.

Janet's Foss is the waterfall to visit before Gordale Scar—a cascade tucked in the woods at the end of a 20-minute trail from Malham. Local legend attributes the cave behind the falls to Janet, queen of Yorkshire's fairies. In summer, people bathe in the natural pool.

Gordale Scar is one of England's most impressive gorges—a 100-meter-high fissure in the limestone, formed by glacial meltwater, which you enter through a narrow opening before discovering an interior waterfall. Turner painted it in 1808. Wordsworth wrote an ode. In dry periods, a moderate scramble up the waterfall allows exit from the top. Wear shoes with good grip.

Malham Cove is the highlight of the Dales journey. This amphitheater-shaped limestone cliff measures 80 meters high and 300 meters wide—formed about 12,000 years ago by a giant glacial waterfall whose water vanished underground in the early 19th century. Climb the 400 steps on the left flank to reach the summit, where you'll find a limestone pavement—a plateau of limestone blocks separated by deep fissures (the 'grykes') that give the whole a lunar appearance. This landscape served as a setting in Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows (Part 1). Peregrine falcons have nested in the cliff since 1993—telescopes are installed below to observe them.

Malham Tarn, 2 kilometers north of the Cove, is a geological anomaly in this limestone landscape: the mountain lake formed on an impermeable Silurian slate layer—the only one in the area—that prevents water infiltration. It's one of Europe's rare high-altitude alkaline lakes and a national nature reserve. A short walk from the road allows a partial circuit.

To finish the day gently, descend toward Settle and the cliffs of Attermire Scar. This long limestone escarpment above town shelters several prehistoric caves including Victoria Cave, excavated in the 19th century—archaeologists found bones of hippopotamus, rhinoceros, and hyena dating to an interglacial period about 120,000 years ago, when Yorkshire had a subtropical climate. The trail from Settle takes 45 minutes one way.

activities.text: The scenic road between Ribblehead, Settle, and Malham is one of northern England's finest—limestone ridges, dry-stone walls, isolated farms, deep valleys. If weather permits, take the B6479 from Ribblehead toward Horton-in-Ribblesdale then the B6265 to Malham for the best scenic route.

The Yorkshire Dales is designated a Dark Sky Reserve—one of England's best-protected low light pollution zones. At night in Malham, far from cities, the Milky Way is visible to the naked eye in clear weather. Stay out half an hour after sunset to watch the sky open up.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire's Most Beautiful Places
Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

visits.text: The smell of smoked herring and marine diesel greets you at the quay. Mallaig is a working port, not a film set: trawlers return in the morning, the Jacobite Steam Train arrives at noon, ferries depart for Skye every hour. The Mallaig Heritage Centre, beside the station, traces the history of herring and rail in 45 minutes.

In 1840, Lord Lovat planted this fishing village wholesale by evicting his tenants from the Loch Morar lands. A hundred years later, Mallaig in the 1960s was Europe's leading herring fishing port. Today it's langoustines and tourism that sustain the village.

activities.text: Board a boat to the Small Isles or take the CalMac ferry to Armadale on Skye—45 minutes crossing with views over Knoydart. Dolphins, porpoises, and basking sharks frequent these waters. Book the car ferry online: morning slots vanish quickly in July-August.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland: Highlands & Skye in 11 days

visits.text: Shivering Mountain: it's not the wind but a 4,000-year-old landslide that justifies the name. Mam Tor's southeast flank - 517 m, ancient Bronze Age fort - still moves several centimetres each year according to the BGS (British Geological Survey). It put an end to the A625 in 1979: the accumulated tarmac layers reached 2 metres thick. 360° view over Hope Valley and Edale.

activities.text: If you set off early from the National Trust car park (S33 8WA), the Great Ridge is often deserted before 9am. The 6.5 km circuit from Castleton takes 3 to 4 hours; the final ascent up Mam Tor is short but steep. Walking poles useful in wet conditions: the limestone gets slippery.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

visits.text: Start at the Science and Industry Museum - it's free, and you're standing on the site of the world's very first passenger railway station, Liverpool Road, opened in 1830. The station and its warehouse are Grade I listed, and it shows. Look for the Manchester Baby: this 5-metre-long monster is a faithful replica of the world's first stored-program computer, built here in 1948. The original was dismantled piece by piece to feed other machines - nothing remains. Volunteers run the replica at fixed times, using the same original vacuum tubes. Note that some galleries are under renovation right now - check the website before you visit.

Take a wander through the Northern Quarter - think Brooklyn or Paris's Marais, colder and more direct. The streets around Oldham Street are plastered with street art, record shops and specialty coffee houses. This is where Factory Records launched Joy Division, New Order and Happy Mondays in 1978. If you like rummaging, Afflecks Palace on Church Street is a sprawling vintage indoor market that captures what Manchester has in its bones.

If you've got an hour to spare, push open the door to the John Rylands Library on Deansgate - entry is free and the neo-Gothic hall from 1900 is worth the trip on its own. In the display cases, you'll find the oldest known page of the New Testament: a 2nd-century papyrus fragment, a few square centimetres that have survived 1,900 years. Few people know this library exists; it's one of Manchester's most astonishing places.

activities.text: Manchester is a city passionate about football to its core. Old Trafford (Manchester United, 74,879 capacity) and the Etihad Stadium (Manchester City, 53,400 capacity) offer guided tours of changing rooms and pitches. If you're lucky enough to catch a match, the atmosphere is unmatched - book tickets several weeks ahead.

Deansgate and the Castlefield area offer plenty of lively pubs for a properly Mancunian evening. Local beers from Marble Brewery or Cloudwater represent the current British craft beer scene at its best.

days_plan.itin_title: England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes
Lake District and North-East Loop: Lakes, Dales and Cathedrals

visits.text: Two neoclassical towers on the Stour's edge, without a church: Mistley Towers are what remains of a church designed by Robert Adam in 1776 for a spa town project that went bankrupt when its backer, Richard Rigby, was ruined. The nave was demolished in 1870; the towers survived as pure historical accident.

Manningtree borders the Stour with its Georgian houses and title of England's smallest town by area—just under 20 hectares. Matthew Hopkins, the 17th-century "Witchfinder General" who sent dozens of women to the gallows, is associated with this area. Manningtree Museum, tiny but well documented, covers this history.

activities.text: The scent of the sea rises from the Stour as you walk The Walls at Manningtree—an estuarine walk along the water to the beach at low tide. Twenty minutes' walk from the town center.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Cross the ancient causeway to St Michael's Mount, Cornwall's own version of France's famous Mont Saint-Michel and one of the most iconic landmarks in England. This tidal island, crowned by a medieval castle and church, has been a site of pilgrimage since Benedictine monks constructed the first church here in 1135. The causeway connecting the island to the mainland at Marazion becomes submerged at high tide, adding an element of adventure to every visit.

Explore the castle atop St Michael's Mount, which evolved from a monastery into a fortress and now serves as a family home while welcoming visitors to its historic rooms and magnificent gardens. The views from the island across Mount's Bay to the Cornish coast are spectacular, encompassing miles of golden beaches and dramatic headlands. The subtropical gardens, clinging to the rocky slopes, feature exotic plants that thrive in the mild Cornish climate.

Stroll through Marazion, the charming mainland town that serves as the gateway to St Michael's Mount and offers excellent views of the island from its beaches and promenade. This historic settlement claims to be one of the oldest chartered towns in Britain, with a history stretching back to the Bronze Age. The traditional pubs and independent shops provide a pleasant base for exploring this corner of Cornwall.

activities.text: Time your visit to cross the causeway on foot at low tide for the full pilgrimage experience

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide
Dark Cornwall: Arthur, Mines and Wreckers 7d

visits.text: Almost nobody stops in Marlborough between Avebury and the M4, which makes it exactly the right place to pause. The High Street is one of the widest in Britain: it served as a market, with livestock, stalls and London-Bath stagecoaches threading their way through. The Great Fire of 1653 destroyed buildings on one side; the arcades of Georgian coaching inns on the north side are the most legible. The Merchant's House (no. 132) is a seventeenth-century silk merchant's dwelling, remarkably preserved. Market on Wednesday and Saturday.

days_plan.itin_title: London to Bath: Surrey, Hampshire & Wiltshire in 10 Days
Deep Wiltshire: from Salisbury to Newbury in 7 days

places.name: Mayfield

visits.text: An archbishop's palace now converted into a Catholic school—such is the fate of Mayfield Palace, medieval residence of the Archbishops of Canterbury since St Dunstan in the tenth century. The fifteenth-century dormitory and fourteenth-century great hall (now the school chapel) are among the finest medieval halls in Sussex. The property is private but visible from the road.

Mayfield's High Street counts 40 officially listed buildings on a single street—a rare concentration even in the Weald. Most date from the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, financed by the profits of the local iron industry. Middle House, built in the second half of the sixteenth century, is now a pub with a carved fireplace.

activities.text: Look for the legend of St Dunstan and the Devil: the archbishop was working as a blacksmith when the Devil, disguised as a beautiful woman, tried to seduce him. Dunstan spotted the cloven hoof and pinched Satan's nose with red-hot tongs. The Devil fled to soothe his burn at the springs of Tunbridge Wells. The tongs are reportedly still kept at Mayfield School.

days_plan.itin_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

map_keywords: Mayfield Palace Old School East Sussex

visits.text: Local walkers simply call it 'the Hill'. White Sheet Hill is an Iron Age fort crowning the ridge north of Mere, with views across three counties on clear days. A Neolithic long barrow on the same summit predates the fort by more than a thousand years. Twenty-minute walk from the town, free parking in Mere. Steep terrain: avoid dress shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Wiltshire: from Salisbury to Newbury in 7 days

visits.text: Locals call the crossing 'the Strood'—a causeway that becomes impassable at spring tides, cutting the island from the mainland for about half an hour. Mersea Island is accessible only by this single road. West Mersea's church is built on a Saxon occupation site dating to around the 7th century, itself atop earlier Roman remains.

The Mersea Museum, right in the village, traces the island's Roman occupation and the history of the oyster industry. The Romans supposedly introduced vines and oysters to this area—both still thrive here. Free entry.

activities.text: Eat oysters at the Company Shed or West Mersea Oyster Bar, two waterfront addresses in West Mersea. Two varieties exist: the Colchester Rock (year-round) and the Colchester Native (September to May). Arrival before 12:30pm recommended on weekends—no reservations at the Company Shed, first come first served.

days_plan.itin_title: 12 Days Through Hertfordshire & Essex: Castles to Coast

visits.text: Discover Mevagissey, the postcard-perfect Cornish fishing village where colorful cottages and working boats crowd around a picturesque double harbor. This authentic fishing community has retained its traditional character while offering excellent opportunities to enjoy the freshest seafood in charming waterfront settings. The narrow lanes climbing up from the harbor reveal independent shops, galleries and glimpses of the fishing heritage that has sustained this village for centuries.

Stroll along the harbor walls watching fishermen land their catch and preparing boats for the next tide, a scene that has changed little over generations. The village's sheltered position and thriving fishing fleet made it a prosperous community, and the handsome merchants' houses climbing the hillside reflect this maritime wealth. Local boat trips offer the chance to explore the coastline from the water and spot the marine wildlife that thrives in these clear Cornish waters.

activities.text: Sample fresh Cornish seafood in one of the harbor-side establishments overlooking the fishing boats

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide

visits.text: Cowdray House—locals just say 'the ruins' as if everyone knows, and everyone does. One of Tudor England's great houses, burned accidentally in 1793 when carpenters forgot to properly extinguish their fire. Henry VIII and Elizabeth I both stayed here. The kitchen tower is the only intact volume. Site managed by the Cowdray Heritage Trust.

Midhurst High Street: too quiet for a town that welcomed Henry VIII on three occasions. Sixteenth- and seventeenth-century timber façades frame a still-functioning market street. The Spread Eagle, an inn on this site since the fifteenth century according to some local sources, is a good stop.

Cowdray House, begun around 1520 by Sir David Owen, presumed half-uncle of Henry VII, in the Cowdray park which still covers several thousand acres. Construction was continued by William FitzWilliam, who in 1533 obtained a royal licence to 'empark' the estate. The Grade I listed ruins were restored and reopened in 2007.

activities.text: Walk along the Rother through Cowdray Park after visiting the ruins: the park is crossed by public footpaths and the ruins reflect in the river from certain angles. Polo is played here in summer—matches open to the public on some weekends.

If you have time, the South Downs Way passes two kilometres south of Midhurst: views over Cowdray park, the heaths and the Sussex plain. An hour's walk return from the centre is enough to understand why this corner of West Sussex attracts walkers.

days_plan.itin_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

places.name: Mumbles

visits.text: Llyn y Fan Fach is a 10-hectare glacial lake set at 510 metres altitude at the foot of the Carmarthen Fans escarpment—the western flanks of the Brecon Beacons. Allow 1h30 drive from Mumbles to get there (via Ammanford), but the site is so little visited and so wild that the journey is more than justified. It's rarely crowded, utterly wild and wrapped in legend: according to Welsh tradition, a fairy would emerge from it to seduce shepherds. One of them married her on condition he would never strike her three times. He broke his promise, she returned to the lake taking all the cattle with her—but her sons became the Physicians of Myddfai, the first documented lineage of herbalist-physicians in Wales, in the 12th century. You reach the lake on foot from the Llanddeusant car park in 40 minutes.

Picws Du (749 m) is the westernmost summit of the Carmarthen Fans ridge. Climb from the lake to reach the knife-edge: on one side the void of the glacial cirque, on the other the grassy steppe descending towards Carmarthenshire. The panorama over Bannau Sir Gâr—the official name of this range of summits since the national park was renamed in 2023—and over the Welsh plain is one of the widest in South Wales.

Llyn y Fan Fawr, the lake's big brother, lies 3 km to the east along the ridge. If you have the energy, continue to this second glacial lake from the summit of Picws Du—the complete loop from Llanddeusant car park is about 13 km and remains fairly isolated, with few walkers.

Mumbles is the kind of place you've never heard of but never want to leave. A seaside village perched at the tip of Swansea Bay, it owes its strange name to the French 'mamelles'—a reference to the two rocky islets at the end of the promontory. This village was once one of the UK's first oyster ports: in 1871, 16 million oysters a year were landed here, shipped by train to London. Disease in the 1920s killed the industry, but the name Oystermouth (the village's old name) remains everywhere. Start with Oystermouth Castle, perched on a hill overlooking the bay—built in the 12th century by the Normans, it's under ongoing restoration.

The Mumbles promenade, between Oyster Wharf and Bracelet Bay, is the village's beating heart. You'll encounter families, cyclists, retirees with dogs. Mumbles Pier opened in 1898 as the terminus of an electric tramway—the world's first fare-paying railway transport network, opened in 1807 with horse traction, converted to steam in 1877 then electrified in 1929 before closing in 1960. Don't miss the pier mouth and the view of Mumbles lighthouse, built in 1793 on the outer islet.

Langland Bay is the first beach west of Mumbles, reached on foot in 30 minutes via the coastal path. Protected by limestone cliffs, it's a sandy beach with restored Victorian bathing huts—a 1920s postcard setting. Surfing is possible here; Caswell Bay, the next bay along (another 20 min walk), is better known for its waves.

activities.text: The Carmarthenshire Fans loop from Llanddeusant car park (13 km route) makes a complete circuit of Llyn y Fan Fach, climbs Picws Du, traverses the ridge to Fan Brycheiniog and descends. It's one of the most solitary and wildest walks in the Brecon Beacons—bring map and compass, mobile signal is non-existent. About 500 m of ascent.

The coastal path between Mumbles and Caswell Bay is a 6 km return walk along limestone cliffs with sweeping views over Swansea Bay, the Mumbles islands and the open sea. The path passes Limeslade Bay and Bracelet Bay before climbing the cliffs to Langland and Caswell. Bring grip shoes—some sections are rocky.

In the evening, settle on the Mumbles seafront to watch the sunset over Swansea Bay. The orange light on the two islets with the white lighthouse is a scene regularly photographed from the end of the pier.

days_plan.itin_title: South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast
South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast

map_keywords: Llyn y Fan Fawr Brecon Beacons

visits.text: Step into the medieval world of Cawdor Castle, forever linked to Shakespeare's Macbeth and still home to the Cawdor family after 600 years. The 14th-century tower house, built around a legendary holly tree, is surrounded by three magnificent gardens and extensive nature trails through ancient woodland. The castle's atmospheric rooms are filled with fascinating artifacts, family portraits and the accumulated treasures of centuries of aristocratic Scottish life.

Explore Brodie Castle, a Z-plan tower house that has been home to the Brodie clan for over 400 years, just 4 miles from Forres. The castle houses an impressive collection of paintings, French furniture and Chinese porcelain, while the Playful Garden delights families with its imaginative design. Special events throughout the year bring the castle and its extensive grounds to life with seasonal celebrations.

Discover the dramatic ruins of Ruthven Barracks near Kingussie, standing stark against the Highland sky as a reminder of the government's attempts to control the Highlands after the 1715 Jacobite Rising. These imposing barracks were captured and burned by Bonnie Prince Charlie's retreating army in 1746, and today the gaunt walls and empty windows create one of Scotland's most evocative historic sites. The views across Strathspey from this elevated position are spectacular.

activities.text: Walk through Cawdor Castle's three distinct gardens: the Walled Garden, Flower Garden and Wild Garden

days_plan.itin_title: Scottish Highlands Road Trip: Castles, Lochs and Wild Landscapes

visits.text: The National Gallery, facing Trafalgar Square, opened in 1824 with just 38 paintings in a townhouse—today its collections hold over 2,300 paintings spanning 700 years of European art. Admission is free, which was revolutionary at the time. Van Gogh's Sunflowers are in Room 43: painted in August 1888 in Arles over one week to decorate his friend Gauguin's bedroom, this painting is one of seven versions in the series—Van Gogh himself declared 'The sunflower is me.' It was acquired by the National Gallery in 1924. Don't miss Leonardo da Vinci's The Virgin of the Rocks, Jan van Eyck's Arnolfini Portrait (1434) and Holbein's The Ambassadors with its famous distorted skull in trompe-l'oeil. The Impressionist room is always packed—arrive at 10am or during lunch. Allow 2 to 3 hours minimum.

activities.text: Free audio guides are available via the National Gallery app—an excellent way to delve deeper into the most important paintings without following a group.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

places.name: National Maritime Museum

visits.text: The National Maritime Museum in Greenwich is the world's largest maritime museum, with nearly 2.5 million objects in its collections. Established by Act of Parliament in 1934 and opened in 1937, it occupies the buildings of the former Royal Hospital School, including the 17th-century Queen's House designed by Inigo Jones. The museum's most moving object—and one of the most poignant in all of England—is the admiral's coat Nelson wore at the Battle of Trafalgar on 21 October 1805. You can still see the bullet hole in the left shoulder of the uniform, at the precise spot where the French marksman from the Redoutable shot him at 1:15pm. The right sleeve is pinned—a reminder that Nelson had lost that arm at the Battle of Santa Cruz de Tenerife in 1795. He died a few hours later knowing that the Franco-Spanish fleet had been defeated. Don't miss the gilded 19-metre state barge built in 1732 for the Prince of Wales, and Turner's largest painting, The Battle of Trafalgar (1824). Make the trip to Greenwich: the area deserves a full half-day, between the museum, the Royal Observatory and the Greenwich meridian.

activities.text: Take advantage of your Greenwich visit to climb to the Royal Observatory—15 minutes on foot uphill from the museum. You can photograph yourself straddling the prime meridian and see John Harrison's H4 marine chronometer, which solved the longitude problem in the 18th century.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

map_keywords: Nelson Trafalgar coat National Maritime Museum

visits.text: The National Portrait Gallery, steps from the National Gallery, reopened in 2023 after a major renovation, presents historical and contemporary portraits of figures who shaped British history—from Queen Elizabeth I to current celebrities. The collection is organized chronologically: start on the top level and descend through the centuries. If you only have limited time, focus on the Tudor and Stuart galleries where you'll find the most striking portraits, including the only painted portrait of Shakespeare from his lifetime.

activities.text: The top-floor restaurant, opened since the 2023 renovation, offers sweeping views over Trafalgar Square and London's rooftops—a good option for lunch after visiting the neighbouring National Gallery.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: The Natural History Museum opened in 1881 in its neo-Romanesque South Kensington building designed by Alfred Waterhouse. Hintze Hall has housed Hope since 2017 - a female blue whale 25.2 metres long (221 bones, 4.5 tonnes) stranded in Wexford harbour, Ireland in March 1891 and purchased by the Museum for £250. She's suspended from the ceiling in a diving plunge posture - the only blue whale in the world presented in this position. In the 1960s, an estimated 400 blue whales remained worldwide; it was in London in 1966 that their commercial hunting was banned. Admission is free.

The Dinosaur galleries (Blue Zone) house some of the most complete fossils ever found in the UK. The Minerals gallery (Red Zone) includes fragments of Martian and lunar meteorites. Two hours minimum to see it properly.

activities.text: South Kensington concentrates three free national museums within a 300-metre radius: the Natural History Museum, Science Museum and Victoria & Albert Museum. If time permits, push on to the V&A - its fashion, design and decorative arts collections are among the world's finest.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries
What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: In clear weather from the lighthouse, the Outer Hebrides—Benbecula, South Uist—cut sharp profiles on the horizon. This is Skye's westernmost point. The 2.2 km return path descends steeply to the lighthouse, lit since 1 November 1909, now automated from Edinburgh.

Neist Point lighthouse has been lit since 1 November 1909, automated since 1990. The surrounding cliffs shelter seasonal gannets, guillemots, and razorbills. At sea: basking sharks, dolphins, porpoises, and occasionally orcas.

activities.text: The car park fills quickly, but the headland stays peaceful: visitors cluster mainly at sunset, one of Skye's most spectacular. From the gate at the top, the path forks—right towards the cliffs for the classic view, left towards the moorland for solitude. No toilets on site.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland: Highlands & Skye in 11 days

visits.text: The Welsh call it the island of the lovers' saint: Llanddwyn is dedicated to Saint Dwynwen, Welsh patron saint of lovers since the 5th century, whose feast day on 25th January corresponds to the 'Welsh Valentine's Day'. The peninsula only becomes an island at the highest tides. You reach it on foot from Newborough car park, through the dunes and forest of the national reserve—about 30 to 45 minutes. The ruins of the medieval chapel, the lighthouse, and the views across to Snowdonia reward the effort.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

visits.text: The museum is free, small, and far better than its address in a Jacobean clothworkers' building might suggest. The West Berkshire Museum documents the two Battles of Newbury from the English Civil War: in September 1643, the Earl of Essex's Parliamentary army halted the Royalist advance in the fields of Wash Common; in October 1644, the second battle around Donnington Castle deepened the Royalist quagmire. Historians consider the First Battle of Newbury a turning point in the Civil War. Open Wednesday, Thursday, Saturday and Sunday, 11am-4pm. Free.

days_plan.itin_title: Deep Wiltshire: from Salisbury to Newbury in 7 days

places.name: Newcastle Upon Tyne

visits.text: Go down to the Quayside and look at the bridges. The Tyne Bridge - the great green arch inaugurated in 1928 by King George V - is Newcastle's icon, and it hides a story almost nobody knows. The same engineering firm, Dorman Long of Middlesbrough, later built Australia's Sydney Harbour Bridge. At the opening, their representative said clearly that Newcastle had served as a 'full-scale trial for the Sydney bridge'. Compare the two photos: the arches are virtually identical. Another detail: at its construction, the Tyne Bridge was Britain's longest steel arch bridge.

Cross the Gateshead Millennium Bridge on foot - it's the world's only tilting bridge of this type, and it's as simple as that. Its two steel arches tilt together like a blinking eye, to let boats pass with 25 metres clearance. The manoeuvre takes 4 minutes and a half: 36,000 people gathered on the riverbanks to watch the first tilt in June 2001. On the other side on the Gateshead bank, the BALTIC Centre for Contemporary Art is in a former flour mill converted in 2002 - it's free, and exhibitions change regularly. Go up to the café on the top floor: you have the best view of the Tyne's seven bridges.

Newcastle Castle Keep deserves 45 minutes - and not because it's grand, but because it's strange. The castle that gave the city its name is today wedged between two Victorian railway viaducts that literally pass through its former bailey. Henry II had it built in 1167 on an earlier Norman fortification.
