Climb to the top of the keep for views over the Tyne and bridges - and to see how thoroughly the Victorians had no qualms about running their trains straight through the middle of a medieval castle.

activities.text: If you want to see Newcastle off the beaten track, head to the Ouseburn Valley - 15 minutes' walk east of the centre. This is the creative quarter: artist studios, micro-breweries, concerts in converted Victorian warehouses. The Victoria Tunnel, a former 19th-century coal transport tunnel dug beneath the city, offers headlamp tours. Book ahead, slots go quickly.

The Quayside in the evening is a Newcastle institution. Bars and restaurants line both banks between the Tyne Bridge and Swing Bridge, and the Geordie atmosphere - that's the nickname for Newcastle residents - is direct and warm. If you want to try local food: the stottie is a thick round bread typical of the region, and pease pudding (split pea purée) is eaten in sandwiches. You won't find this anywhere else in England.

days_plan.itin_title: England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes
Lake District and North-East Loop: Lakes, Dales and Cathedrals
Yorkshire and North East England: Dales, Castles and Coastal Drama

map_keywords: BALTIC Centre for Contemporary Art Gateshead

visits.text: Ride the waves at Newquay, England's undisputed surf capital, where Atlantic swells create some of the best surfing conditions in Britain. Whether you're an experienced surfer or complete beginner, numerous surf schools and board rental shops cater to all abilities on the town's many beaches. The surf culture has shaped Newquay's laid-back character and vibrant nightlife, making it a favorite destination for active visitors.

Explore the dramatic coastline around Newquay, where low-slung cliffs and hidden coves create perfect conditions for coasteering adventures combining climbing, jumping and swimming. Fistral Beach, consistently ranked among the best surf beaches in Europe, hosts international competitions that draw the world's top surfers. The surrounding headlands offer spectacular walking with views along the coast in both directions.

Discover the family-friendly attractions around Newquay, including the excellent zoo and several National Trust manor houses set in the rolling countryside slightly inland. As one of the larger towns in Cornwall, Newquay offers a wider range of accommodation, restaurants and entertainment than smaller coastal villages. The permanent population of 20,000 can multiply five times during peak summer season, a testament to the town's enduring popularity.

activities.text: Book a surf lesson on Fistral Beach to experience the thrill of riding Cornish waves

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide

visits.text: Northallerton is a North Yorkshire market town you might pass through without stopping - but its lively High Street offers a welcome contrast with the wild landscapes before and after. The town is mentioned in the Domesday Book of 1086; the Battle of the Standard, in 1138, was fought two miles north.

activities.text: Strategic lunch stop on the High Street before the remaining 150 km to the Lake District. The Golden Lion, a former coaching inn on the main street, serves Yorkshire dishes at reasonable prices. Northallerton is also known for its independent shops - a coffee and short break do you good.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

places.name: Norwich

visits.text: Norwich Castle dominates the city from arrival: its Norman keep sits on an artificial mound above the centre, visible from everywhere. William the Conqueror ordered its construction as early as 1067—98 Saxon houses were razed to clear the site. The stone keep, built between 1094 and 1121 in limestone imported from Caen, is one of the most decorated of the Norman era: its blind arcading is exceptional. After a five-year restoration, it reopened in 2025 with the interior recreated in its twelfth-century state—it's a good time to visit. The museum inside deserves time: it houses notably the paintings of the Norwich School and, an anecdote not to be missed, Britain's largest collection of ceramic teapots.

Norwich Cathedral is a Norman cathedral begun in 1096 at the same time as the castle. Its 96-metre spire is the second-tallest in England after Salisbury. The cloisters are the largest in England and they house more than 400 medieval boss sculptures in the vaults—look up and take time to decipher them: you see scenes from the Apocalypse, fantastic animals and biblical episodes of remarkable quality. The cathedral was built using limestone imported from Caen, carried by barge from the River Wensum to the foot of the construction site via the water gate of Pull's Ferry, whose remains are still visible today.

Norwich Market is one of the largest and oldest open-air markets in England, with nearly 200 stalls. It was established here by the Normans in the eleventh century and has never moved since. Right next door, Elm Hill is Norwich's best-preserved medieval street: its fifteenth- and sixteenth-century timber-framed houses now house antique dealers, galleries and cafés. In late afternoon, the oblique light on the cobbles and leaning façades is particularly beautiful.

Take twenty minutes to visit the Sainsbury Centre for Visual Arts on the University of East Anglia campus—admission is free and the building alone is worth the trip. Norman Foster designed it in 1978: a great hangar of aluminium and glass that was a revolutionary piece of architecture at the time. Inside, you'll find African sculptures, Etruscan bronzes and works by Giacometti, Francis Bacon and Henry Moore presented in the same space, without partitions—a deliberately unusual arrangement. If you like art or architecture, you won't regret the stop.

activities.text: Evening in the Norwich Lanes, the quarter of independent lanes between the market and the cathedral. This is where the historic pubs, restaurants and the town's most original shops are concentrated. If you like craft beers, several pubs serve the local productions of Norfolk's breweries.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Road Trip: Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridge
From Cambridge to London: Eastern England in 5 Days

map_keywords: Sainsbury Centre for Visual Arts Norwich

visits.text: Notting Hill draws its character from its complex social history: a poor neighbourhood in the 1950s, it welcomed a large Caribbean community who launched the Notting Hill Carnival in 1966 - today Europe's largest street carnival with over a million participants each late August. The pastel facades you see resulted from 1980s-90s gentrification. Portobello Road is the market's main street: on Saturdays, antiques and bric-a-brac stalls extend nearly a kilometre from Notting Hill Gate to Golborne Road. Avoid Saturdays if you hate crowds.

activities.text: The Electric Cinema on Portobello Road is one of Britain's oldest working cinemas - opened in 1910. Its interior preserves its leather armchairs and two-seater sofas. Westbourne Grove, the street parallel to Portobello, concentrates the neighbourhood's best independent fashion and design shops - more local atmosphere, less touristy.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

places.name: Nottingham

visits.text: The real medieval castle of Nottingham was demolished by Cromwell after the Civil War - what stands today is a 17th-century ducal palace converted into a museum. Don't come for a castle: come for the caves. Beneath the building, a network of medieval tunnels carved into the red sandstone burrows into the rock - dungeons, wine cellars, secret passages, some occupied since Anglo-Saxon times. The underground guided tour is the best thing to do here.

The Lace Market is the former heart of the global lace industry - in the 19th century, Nottingham manufactured the machine lace worn throughout Europe. The red brick Victorian warehouses are now bars, galleries, and restaurants. St Mary's Church, in Perpendicular style, is the church where Robin Hood supposedly married Maid Marian according to legend. The National Justice Museum, in a former Lace Market courthouse, displays 14th-century courtrooms and dungeons from 1449.

Sherwood Forest, 30 km north, is home to the Major Oak: England's largest oak tree, estimated at 800 to 1,000 years old, 10 meters in girth, 28 meters canopy spread, 23 tons. Its branches have been supported by metal frameworks for decades. The forest that once covered a quarter of Nottinghamshire is reduced today to a 420-hectare nature reserve - but the trail from the visitor center to the tree takes 15 minutes and the atmosphere of the old oak forest is real.

activities.text: Ye Olde Trip to Jerusalem is carved directly into the sandstone rock beneath the castle - rooms hewn by hand, low ceilings, with raw rock ceiling above the tables. The sign claims 1189 as its founding date, when crusaders stopped here before joining Richard the Lionheart. The exact date is debated, but the pub has existed at least since the 17th century and the underground atmosphere is perfectly authentic. Stop here for a drink before hitting the road.

To return: direct train from Nottingham Station to London St Pancras in 1h40, frequency every hour. The M1 motorway links Nottingham to London in about 2h depending on traffic. East Midlands airport is 20 km southwest of the city - connections to major European cities.

days_plan.itin_title: The Midlands and Peak District Essentials
Midlands and Peak District Road Trip: Heritage and Wild Landscapes

map_keywords: Ye Olde Trip to Jerusalem Nottingham

visits.text: A castle without a keep, a collection of royal horseshoes, and a centuries-old tradition: Oakham Castle is one of England's curiosities. The Norman Great Hall, built around 1180–1190, houses over 200 horseshoes given by visiting nobles and monarchs—tradition dictates that every peer of the realm passing through Oakham must give a horseshoe to the lord. Free entry.

Rutland Water glistens east of town—a 1,255-hectare reservoir that's one of Western Europe's largest artificial lakes. Created in the 1970s, it now attracts yachts, birdwatchers, and cyclists who complete the circuit (38km). The bird observation centre at Egleton is particularly active in winter.

activities.text: You cycle around the reservoir—or part of it—on the marked cycle path along the shores. Bikes are available at Whitwell on the north shore. In autumn, geese and migrant ducks arrive by the thousands. The view from the north shore, with Normanton church in the water, is Rutland's postcard.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia, Cambridge & Rutland: 13-Day Road Trip

visits.text: The CalMac ferry from Castlebay to Oban takes about 5 hours - long enough to feel like a proper sea passage, with the Small Isles of Eigg, Rum and Muck visible to the north and the Mull coast emerging to the east as you approach. This is one of the most scenic ferry routes in Scotland. Book as early as possible - this route is popular and cabins sell out. Arrive in Oban in the early evening. The town is the main ferry hub for the west coast islands and earns its nickname 'gateway to the Isles' with something between efficiency and charm.

McCaig's Tower, the circular stone colosseum that crowns the hill above Oban, was built between 1895 and 1902 by local banker John Stuart McCaig as a memorial to his family and as a way of providing winter employment for local stone masons. He died before the interior was completed. It's never been anything but a shell, but the walk up from the harbour takes 10 minutes and the view over the bay and the island of Kerrera is worth it, particularly in the evening light.

Dunstaffnage Castle, 3 km north of Oban, is a 13th-century royal fortress that once held the Stone of Destiny before it was moved to Scone. Robert the Bruce captured it in 1308. Flora MacDonald - the woman who helped Bonnie Prince Charlie escape after Culloden by disguising him as her maidservant - was briefly imprisoned here in 1746. The ruin sits on a rock above Loch Etive and takes about 45 minutes to visit properly.

The drive south from Oban along the A816, or inland via the A85 towards Glasgow, passes through some of the most beautiful landscape in Argyll. If you have time before departing, the Bridge over the Atlantic at Seil Island - a tiny hump-backed stone arch over the narrow Clachan Sound, built in 1792 - is the only road bridge to cross the Atlantic and takes about 20 minutes to reach from Oban. The village of Easdale just beyond is the smallest permanently inhabited island in the Inner Hebrides.

activities.text: Oban Distillery, at the bottom of Stafford Street in the town centre, has been producing single malt since 1794, making it one of the oldest distilleries in Scotland. The visitor experience is good - the distillery is surprisingly compact given how central it is - and the 14-year-old expression is one of the defining West Highland malts. Book a tour in advance.

The Oban seafood hut on the pier - a converted CalMac waiting room - is one of the best places in Scotland to eat langoustines, dressed crab and cold-smoked salmon, and it is completely unpretentious about it. It has no tables inside and the seating faces the ferry terminal. Queue, order, find a bollard to sit on. This is the right way to end the ferry journey.

A short passenger ferry (10 minutes, bike-friendly) crosses to the island of Kerrera directly opposite Oban harbour. The island has no cars, a ruined MacDougall castle at its southern end and a tea garden halfway round a 6 km walking circuit of the island. It's a complete contrast to the busy mainland ferry port and a pleasant way to spend a last half-day before driving south.

days_plan.itin_title: Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain
Outer Hebrides Road Trip: Lewis, Harris and the Island Chain

visits.text: Local legend claims Old Harry takes its name from Harry Paye, Poole pirate who died in 1419—or perhaps from the Devil's English nickname. Three chalk stacks detached from Handfast Point by erosion, marking the eastern end of the Jurassic Coast. The first 'wife' of Old Harry collapsed in 1896.

Walk from Studland village, about a mile (20 minutes) on flat path along the South West Coast Path. On clear days you spot the Needles on the Isle of Wight in the distance—remnants of the same chalk ridge that once connected Purbeck to the island. Cliffs are unstable; keep children close.

activities.text: The Bankes Arms in Studland serves local ales in a sprawling garden with sea views, perfect lunch stop before reclaiming the car. South Beach car park is free for National Trust members, otherwise paid.

days_plan.itin_title: England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton

visits.text: Storr comes from Old Norse, likely meaning 'great': the Vikings named this 49 m pinnacle long before the postcards. Legend says a buried giant left his thumb protruding from the ground. The site was born from a massive landslip on the Trotternish ridge. Car park £6, 7 miles north of Portree.

The pinnacles surge from the mist like teeth: in overcast weather, the Old Man takes on a gothic dimension that sunshine never gives it. The ascent takes 45 minutes on a 2.3 km return trail. At the top, views over the Sound of Raasay and the mainland.

activities.text: 2.3 km return, 45 minutes at normal pace—reasonable, but the final slope is steep. Crowds peak between 10 AM and 3 PM. Arrive at opening for morning light and fewer people. Waterproof footwear mandatory.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland: Highlands & Skye in 11 days

visits.text: Twelve sides, none round: Orford Castle is the only surviving polygonal keep of this type in England. Built by Henry II from 1165, it dominates the village and sea from its 30-metre keep. From the roof, the view over Orford Ness—a spit of shingle and sparse vegetation protected by the National Trust—is striking. The 'Wild Man of Orford', a merman captured here in the twelfth century, is part of local folklore.

You take the ferry to Orford Ness, an isolated shingle spit that was a classified military testing ground throughout the twentieth century. Abandoned experimental buildings survive among the vegetation, creating a unique atmosphere. The National Trust manages the site and runs regular crossings from Orford quay.

activities.text: Butley Creek oysters, smoked shrimps, and North Sea crab: Orford quay is where you eat these specialities direct from the fishermen. The Butley Orford Oysterage, founded in the 1950s, still smokes its herrings and eels on site. Avoid summer weekends—tables fill up fast.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia, Cambridge & Rutland: 13-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Oundle is one of Northamptonshire's best-preserved market towns—its streets are lined with honey-coloured limestone houses that give the centre a provincial Cambridge air. The town has for centuries hosted Oundle School, founded in 1556, whose buildings blend into the urban fabric. The Thursday morning market is one of the region's oldest.

St Peter's Church dominates the town with its spire—Northamptonshire's tallest at 64 metres. There has been a church on this site since the eighth century, but the current building dates mainly from the thirteenth to fifteenth centuries. The Venerable Bede already mentions a monastery at Oundle in 709. The interior is accessible outside services.

activities.text: You head down to the River Nene through the meadows of Barnwell Country Park, two kilometres south of Oundle. This nature park around former lakes offers flat paths and waterfowl observation. Lyveden New Bield—an unfinished sixteenth-century manor, 8km away—is worth a detour for Elizabethan history enthusiasts.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia, Cambridge & Rutland: 13-Day Road Trip

visits.text: Oxford without Christ Church or Blenheim—this is where you realize what the rest of the route won't be. Favor the streets of Jericho and the Ashmolean Museum (founded 1683, collection of 120,000 objects) before heading to the banks of the Cherwell. In the evening, the pubs along the lanes around St Giles close early—arrive before 9pm.

activities.text: The scent of damp stone rises from the Cherwell's banks at dusk—that's when you take a punt. Hire a flat-bottomed boat from Cherwell Boathouse or Magdalen Bridge (around £20/hr, no booking needed on weekday evenings). Minimal effort, maximum effect.

Use the morning for the Covered Market or the banks of the Isis before catching the train to London Paddington (55 min). Best cafés for waiting out a delayed train: The Missing Bean on Turl Street, or the Ashmolean café.

days_plan.itin_title: Thames & Vale of White Horse: 6 days Loop
Thames & Vale of White Horse: 6 days Loop

visits.text: Oxford Street is Europe's busiest shopping street - around 500,000 visitors daily at peak times. The street follows the route of an old Roman road and took on its commercial vocation in the 18th century. Selfridges (opened in 1909 by American Harry Gordon Selfridge, who introduced the concept of decorative window displays to London) is the most emblematic - its Christmas windows are an annual event. Avoid Saturday afternoons if you're allergic to crowds.

activities.text: Bond Street, a parallel street to the north, is the address of fashion houses and jewellers - Cartier, Tiffany, Burberry, Chanel have their flagship stores there. Mayfair, the residential neighbourhood south of Bond Street, is one of the world's most expensive in real estate terms and one of the finest for an aimless stroll. If you play Monopoly, you're on the board's most expensive square.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Padstow is nicknamed 'Padstein' by Cornish locals, a nod to Rick Stein, who opened his first restaurant on the quay in 1975 and has steadily expanded his empire ever since: The Seafood Restaurant, St Petroc's Bistro, Rick Stein's Café, the fishmonger on the harbour, the cookery school, and even a pub in the neighbouring village of St Merryn. The Seafood Restaurant remains the heart of the operation—it received the AA Food Service Award 2024 for hospitality standards. The menu changes with the catch, but expect lobster thermidor, turbot hollandaise and seafood platters. Book well ahead in summer.

Padstow harbour is steps from the restaurant: you'll see fishing boats unloading their catch directly on the quay, and Stein's fishmonger stocks the same species served in the restaurants—turbot from Trevose Head, lobsters and crabs from the Boscastle Belle. If you want ultra-fresh without fine dining, stop by the fishmonger one morning.

About forty kilometres south, Newlyn fish market is one of Britain's busiest fishing ports. The daily auction happens early morning—early risers can watch the bidding for mackerel, sardines, sea bass and scallops. It's the true starting point of Cornwall's fish supply chain.

activities.text: Stein's cookery school in Padstow offers one- or two-day courses focused on Cornish seafood: how to choose a whole fish, fillet it, prepare a court-bouillon or cook a lobster. The level is open to all. It's one of the best ways to understand why Rick Stein has been repeating for 50 years that 'nothing is more exhilarating than fresh fish simply cooked'.

North Cornwall coast oysters are among the country's finest—order them fresh, plain or with a squeeze of lemon, at The Seafood Restaurant's oyster bar or from harbour stalls. It's the most direct way to taste the Cornish sea.

days_plan.itin_title: England's Gastronomic Trail: Markets, Pubs & Culinary Heritage

visits.text: Penshurst Place was granted in 1552 by Edward VI to Sir William Sidney, his tutor and steward—the castle had previously served as a hunting lodge for Henry VIII during his courtship of Anne Boleyn (Hever Castle is six kilometers away). The Sidney family still owns it, making it one of England's oldest family homes. The 14th-century Baron's Hall is one of the rare examples of a medieval domestic great hall still intact.

The Sidneys extinguished their male line in the 18th century but their heirs took up the name—today the De L'Isles—and continue to manage Penshurst. Queen Elizabeth I stayed here on several occasions and the Queen Elizabeth Room bears her name. There's also a Toy Museum with toys from successive generations of Sidneys, including a doll's house and 19th-century lead soldiers.

activities.text: Explore the 11 acres of walled gardens at Penshurst, divided into 'rooms' by more than a mile of yew hedges—a structure resembling what Vita Sackville-West would create at Sissinghurst a generation later. The rose beds in June and tulips in April-May are highlights. The heritage apple orchard is also worth a detour in autumn.

days_plan.itin_title: Surrey to Kent: Castles and Gardens in 10 Days

visits.text: Journey to Land's End, the most westerly point of mainland England, where dramatic cliffs plunge into the Atlantic Ocean and the views extend to the Isles of Scilly on clear days. This legendary location has captivated visitors since ancient times; even the Greeks knew this place, naming it 'Belerion' meaning 'place of the sun.' The famous signpost marking distances to faraway destinations has become an iconic photo opportunity for visitors from around the world.

Experience the Minack Theatre, one of the most unique and spectacular performance venues in the world, carved into the clifftop overlooking Porthcurno Bay. Nearly 100 years ago, Rowena Cade began chiseling this remarkable amphitheatre out of the granite cliff face, creating an open-air stage with the Atlantic Ocean as its backdrop. Even when no performances are scheduled, visiting the theatre to admire its ingenious design and magnificent setting is worthwhile.

Explore the golden sands of Porthcurno Beach, consistently rated among the finest beaches in the UK, where turquoise waters lap against dramatic cliffs. The beach's sheltered position and crystal-clear waters make it popular for swimming, while the surrounding cliffs offer excellent walking. The nearby Telegraph Museum tells the fascinating story of the undersea cables that once connected Britain to the world from this remote Cornish cove.

activities.text: Catch an evening performance at the Minack Theatre for an unforgettable cultural experience

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide
England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes
Dark Cornwall: Arthur, Mines and Wreckers 7d

visits.text: Piccadilly Circus was created in 1819 at the junction of Piccadilly and Regent Street, laid out by John Nash for the Prince Regent. The Shaftesbury Memorial Fountain at the centre - often mistaken for Eros - actually represents the Angel of Christian Charity. It was inaugurated in 1893 and is the first statue cast in aluminium in London. The giant illuminated advertising panels appeared in the 1910s; they were switched off during both world wars for blackout. Piccadilly Circus is far livelier in the evening than during the day.

activities.text: From Piccadilly Circus, Carnaby Street is a 5-minute walk north - symbol of Swinging London in the 1960s, when fashion boutiques and musicians like the Rolling Stones converged there. The surrounding network of alleys (Kingly Court, Newburgh Street) retains an independent character. More elegant, Regent Street deserves a walk for its Georgian architecture.

Leicester Square, a 3-minute walk east, is the heart of the West End's theatre and cinema district. The TKTS booth on the square sells discounted tickets for evening shows on the day - if you want to see a musical without paying full price, come in early afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: The heather turns purple in August across the North York Moors - 554 km² of open moorland, one of the largest heather moorlands in the UK. The road from Pickering to Whitby via Saltersgate crosses breathtaking ridges. The views are most intense between Fylingdales and Goathland.

activities.text: The North Yorkshire Moors Railway crosses 18 miles of moorland by steam locomotive between Pickering and Whitby. It's one of England's finest heritage lines, also used as backdrop in several TV productions. A single journey takes 1h30 - possibility to get off at Goathland (Aidensfield in Heartbeat) and catch a later train.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

visits.text: Leaving Edinburgh northwards, stop at Blackness Castle on the shores of the Firth of Forth — a 15th-century fortress shaped like a stone ship, sitting right on the waterfront. It has served as a state prison, royal arsenal and filming location for the Outlander series. Admission is modest, the view across the Forth justifies the stop.

The Kelpies at Falkirk are what you see from the motorway without expecting them: two steel horse heads 30 metres tall, the height of an eight-storey building. Glaswegian sculptor Andy Scott completed them in October 2013 — each weighs 300 tonnes and is built from 30,000 hand-assembled steel pieces. The name comes from Scottish folklore: kelpies are shape-shifting water horses said to possess the strength of ten normal horses. In practice, the sculptures pay tribute to the Clydesdale horses that hauled barges along Scotland's canals for centuries. Access to The Helix park is free — nearly a million people visited in their first year.

Stirling Castle dominates Scotland's central plain from a basalt outcrop exactly as Edinburgh Castle dominates its city — the two fortresses controlled the country's two strategic gateways. This is where Mary Queen of Scots was crowned in 1543 at nine months old, and where James VI, future James I of England, spent his childhood. The Great Hall from 1503, restored in its original canary yellow, is one of Scotland's largest surviving medieval halls. From the ramparts on clear days, you can see both the Wallace Monument and Bannockburn battlefield — two of the great sites of Scottish independence.

Doune Castle, 15 km north of Stirling, merits a detour if you have an hour. This 14th-century fortress served as the filming location for Monty Python and the Holy Grail (1974) — the scenes where knights imitate horses with coconuts were shot here — and more recently for Game of Thrones and Outlander. Audio guides are available: one of them is narrated by Terry Jones himself, Monty Python member.

Blair Castle at Blair Atholl has been the Murray clan's seat for over seven centuries — the first stones date to 1269, when a Comyn clan lord began building on land that didn't belong to him. Its 30 rooms span 750 years of history. What strikes you immediately is the Ballroom: the ceiling and walls are decorated with 175 pairs of deer antlers, making it one of Scotland's most peculiar rooms. Look for the baroque entrance hall with its arsenal of muskets and broadswords used at the Battle of Culloden in 1746 — the castle was besieged by Jacobites during this final act of the rebellion, in the last siege conducted on British soil. Queen Victoria's visit in 1844 had unexpected consequences: so impressed by the castle guard, she granted the Atholl Highlanders the unique right to maintain a private army — the only private regiment still active in Europe today.

Blair Athol Distillery sits at the entrance to Pitlochry — you can't miss it with its grey granite buildings. Founded in 1798, it's one of the oldest working distilleries in the Highlands. The water feeding production descends from Ben Vrackie through peat moorland, giving the whisky its characteristic spicy and lightly smoky notes. The tour includes a tasting and takes you through the fermentation rooms and bonded warehouses.

Make the detour to Queen's View following the B8019 west of Pitlochry — it's a viewpoint above Loch Tummel with one of the Highlands' most reproduced vistas. The loch stretches between wooded hills, with Schiehallion (a conical 1,083-metre peak that looks like a child's drawing) as backdrop. The name supposedly comes either from Queen Victoria who stopped here in 1866, or from Isabella of Scotland, wife of Robert the Bruce — both accounts circulate. The car park is paid but the view is free.

The Pass of Killiecrankie, 5 km north of Pitlochry on the A9, is a wooded gorge where the River Garry rushes between rock faces covered in forest. This is where in 1689 Viscount Dundee defeated government troops in the first Jacobite uprising — but he was himself killed during the victory, which sealed the movement's failure. A fleeing English soldier allegedly jumped 5.5 metres across the river to escape the Highlanders: the spot is still called the Soldier's Leap. The path along the gorge floor from the visitor centre is flat and takes 45 minutes round trip.

activities.text: The scenic A9 route to the Highlands from Stirling is one of the journey's finest transitions: within a few dozen kilometres, farmland gives way to the wooded hills of Perthshire, then to the first Highland peaks. Take a break in Dunkeld, a small cathedral town on the River Tay — the seven-arched bridge from 1809 and the medieval cathedral ruins merit 30 minutes.

Walk along the River Tummel from Pitlochry: the wooded trail running from the town centre to Pitlochry Dam is flat, easy and gives access to the salmon ladder. From April to October, you can watch Atlantic salmon climb the 34 stepped pools built in the 1950s when the dam was erected — a compromise between hydroelectricity and wildlife conservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye
Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye
Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye
Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye
Haunted Scotland: 7 Days in the Land of Ghosts

visits.text: Plockton is arguably the most unexpected village in the Highlands: thanks to the Gulf Stream's influence, Cordyline australis—New Zealand palms—grow along the waterfront, planted in the 19th century. The village was founded in 1801 as a herring port by Sir Hugh Innes. At its 1841 peak, it had over 500 inhabitants; today around 470. The BBC drama Hamish Macbeth, starring Robert Carlyle, was filmed here in the 1990s. At low tide, you can walk to Eilean nan Gamhainn, an islet that briefly connects to the village. Stroll along Harbour Street and dine in one of the pubs that often feature traditional music in the evenings.

10 km east of Plockton, don't miss Eilean Donan Castle, one of Scotland's most photographed images. Set on an islet at the confluence of three lochs (Loch Duich, Loch Long, and Loch Alsh) and connected to shore by a stone arch bridge, it was built in the 13th century to defend the lands of Kintail against the Vikings. In 1719, 46 Spanish soldiers supporting the Jacobite cause were besieged here by three Royal Navy frigates—the 343 barrels of gunpowder stored within its walls were used to blow it up. It was purchased in 1911 and rebuilt in 1932. Highlander (1986) and the James Bond film The World Is Not Enough (1999) were filmed here. Admission charged—inside, the banquet halls with their 4-meter-thick walls are worth as much as the exterior setting.

activities.text: Boat trips from Plockton's pontoon allow observation of common seals and otters in Loch Carron. If you're there at low tide, the coral beach 25 minutes' walk north of the village is a cove of white sand and translucent water—one of the area's finest surprises. Free access.

days_plan.itin_title: Scottish Highlands Road Trip: Castles, Lochs and Wild Landscapes
Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye

visits.text: The Eden Project, in a former kaolin quarry 15km from St Austell, was built between 1998 and 2001 for £141 million. Two ETFE bubble biomes – 100 times lighter than glass – house the world's largest indoor tropical biome (55 meters high, 200 meters long) and a Mediterranean biome. The hexagonal bubble design was sketched on a pub napkin in 1996. The biomes were used as a set in James Bond film 'Die Another Day.' Book online.

The Lost Gardens of Heligan, near Mevagissey, represent Europe's largest garden restoration. Before World War I, 22 gardeners maintained these 200 acres for the Tremayne family. Sixteen died in combat, and the gardens were abandoned for 70 years – until their rediscovery in 1990. Restoration began in 1991. Don't miss the Jungle with its giant tree ferns, and the pineapple pit – the only one in Europe still operating.

Plymouth Hoe is the grand esplanade overlooking Plymouth Sound. Here Francis Drake supposedly finished his game of bowls before confronting the Spanish Armada in 1588. The Mayflower Steps in the Barbican mark the embarkation point of the 102 Pilgrims aboard the Mayflower on 6 September 1620. The Pilgrims weren't meant to pass through Plymouth; a Channel storm forced them here.

activities.text: Boat trips from the Mayflower Steps explore Plymouth Sound and cruise up the River Tamar to Devonport Naval Dockyard. Companies offer guided tours lasting 1.5 to 2 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: England Grand Tour: From Cornwall to the Northern Lakes

visits.text: Explore Poole, home to the largest natural harbor in Europe, where centuries of maritime history blend with modern watersports and a thriving yachting scene. The historic quay bustles with boat trips, waterfront restaurants and the departure point for ferries to Brownsea Island. This ancient port has welcomed traders and travelers since Roman times, and its sheltered waters now attract paddleboarders, kitesurfers and sailors from across Britain.

Discover Sandbanks, the exclusive peninsula at the entrance to Poole Harbour that reportedly has one of the highest land values by area in the world. This millionaires' playground features stunning beaches backed by some of Britain's most expensive properties, yet the golden sands and clear waters remain accessible to all visitors. The views across to Brownsea Island and the dramatic cliffs of the Jurassic Coast make this one of Dorset's most scenic spots.

Take a boat tour from Poole to see Old Harry Rocks, the dramatic chalk stacks that mark the eastern end of the UNESCO World Heritage Jurassic Coast. These gleaming white pillars rising from the turquoise sea have been sculpted by millennia of wind and waves, creating one of England's most iconic coastal landmarks. The boat trip offers unique perspectives on the cliffs and coastline impossible to appreciate from land.

activities.text: Try paddleboarding or kitesurfing in the sheltered waters of Europe's largest natural harbor

days_plan.itin_title: Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks

places.name: Port Isaac

visits.text: Fall in love with Port Isaac, a quintessential Cornish fishing village snuggled around a tiny harbor in North Cornwall. A cluster of whitewashed and slate-fronted cottages tumble down steep lanes to the pocket-sized beach where colorful fishing boats bob at their moorings. The village's protected harbor has preserved its historic character, even as the fishing industry that built it has declined, creating one of the most photogenic destinations in Cornwall.

Explore Port Isaac's narrow lanes and atmospheric corners that have featured in numerous film and television productions, including the popular Doc Martin series. The village's history is intimately tied to fishing, an industry that peaked here in the early 19th century when pilchards were exported across Europe. Today, traditional fishing continues alongside a thriving tourism industry, with crab and lobster pots stacked on the quay.

Walk the spectacular stretch of the South West Coast Path connecting Port Isaac to Tintagel, a 10-mile route that takes in dramatic rock formations and lesser-visited beaches. This section of Cornwall's coastline is particularly spectacular in spring when wildflowers carpet the clifftops, adding splashes of color to the green headlands. The walking offers constantly changing perspectives on the rugged North Cornwall coast.

activities.text: Watch for the Fisherman's Friends, the local sea shanty group who sometimes perform on the harbor's Platt

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide

map_keywords: South West Coast Path Port Isaac

visits.text: Discover the Lizard Peninsula, one of the most spectacular stretches of coastline in all of England and a personal favorite for many visitors to Cornwall. Thanks to limited road access and minimal development, this southernmost point of mainland Britain retains an unspoiled character and get-away-from-it-all atmosphere that feels increasingly rare. The peninsula's serpentine rock creates distinctive green and red coloring in the cliffs and has been worked by local craftspeople for centuries.

Visit the Cornish Seal Sanctuary, where injured and orphaned seals receive treatment and rehabilitation before being released back into the wild. Unlike typical zoo attractions, this facility focuses primarily on rescue and recovery, giving visitors the opportunity to see these charismatic marine mammals while supporting important conservation work. The sanctuary offers insight into the challenges facing Cornwall's seal population and the dedicated efforts to protect them.

Explore the idyllic fishing villages of Coverack and Cadgwith, where thatched cottages cluster around tiny harbors that have changed little over generations. These authentic communities offer perfect spots to enjoy fish and chips while watching the fishing boats come and go. The dramatic coastal scenery surrounding these villages, with towering cliffs and hidden coves, rewards those who venture off the main tourist routes.

Marvel at Kynance Cove, often considered one of the most beautiful beaches in Cornwall with its turquoise waters, white sand and dramatic serpentine rock formations. This stunning cove, owned by the National Trust, offers excellent swimming when conditions allow and spectacular photography opportunities year-round. The unique geology creates islands and caves that are revealed at low tide, adding an element of exploration to any visit.

activities.text: Walk the South West Coast Path along the Lizard's dramatic clifftops with views toward Lizard Point

days_plan.itin_title: Cornwall Coastal Road Trip: Castles, Secret Coves & Celtic Heritage Guide

visits.text: Explore the Isle of Portland, the most southerly point of Dorset, a limestone peninsula joined to the mainland by Chesil Beach and steeped in military history. The biggest man-made harbor in the world, of critical importance during both World Wars, speaks to Portland's strategic significance, while today the island offers tranquility, abundant wildlife and dramatic coastal scenery. The distinctive Portland stone quarried here has been used to construct some of Britain's finest buildings, including St Paul's Cathedral.

Visit Portland Bill Lighthouse, the striking red and white tower that has guided ships around this dangerous headland since 1906. The lighthouse offers tours revealing the workings of this vital navigational aid, while the surrounding cliffs provide excellent opportunities for birdwatching and coastal photography. The views from Portland Bill extend across Lyme Bay, with the outline of Devon visible on clear days.

Discover Portland Castle, one of England's best-preserved coastal fortresses from the reign of Henry VIII, built in the 1540s to defend against French and Spanish invasion. This artillery castle, with its distinctive rounded walls designed to deflect cannonballs, tells the story of Tudor England's precarious position in European politics. The castle later served as a private residence and now offers fascinating insights into 500 years of English history.

activities.text: Visit Pulpit Rock, the dramatic coastal feature near the lighthouse, and watch the waves crash against the stone platform

days_plan.itin_title: Dorset's Jurassic Coast Guide: Cliffs, Corfe Castle & Coastal Walks

visits.text: Enter the whimsical world of Portmeirion, an enchanting Italianate village on the North Wales coast that feels like a slice of the Amalfi Coast transported to the shores of Snowdonia. Designed by visionary architect Sir Clough Williams-Ellis between 1925 and 1975, this unique village features colorful buildings, campaniles, and piazzas nestled in subtropical gardens cascading to the Dwyryd Estuary. The pastel-painted facades and Mediterranean architecture create an otherworldly atmosphere unlike anywhere else in Britain.

Wander through Portmeirion's labyrinthine paths discovering hidden courtyards, ornate fountains and architectural salvage from across Europe incorporated into Williams-Ellis's grand design. The village gained worldwide fame as the setting for the cult 1960s television series 'The Prisoner,' and fans still visit to explore the iconic locations. Every corner reveals unexpected details, from classical statues to whimsical follies, creating endless opportunities for exploration.

Explore the extensive woodland gardens surrounding the village, where exotic plants thrive in the mild coastal climate created by the Gulf Stream. The wild gardens, planted with rhododendrons, hydrangeas and rare specimens from around the world, offer miles of scenic coastal walks along the estuary. The views across to Snowdonia's peaks provide a stunning backdrop to this unique architectural fantasy.

activities.text: Take a scenic coastal walk through the woodland gardens with views across the estuary to Snowdonia

days_plan.itin_title: Wales Road Trip: Conwy Castle, Pembrokeshire Coast & Snowdonia
Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

places.name: Portree

visits.text: Stop at Urquhart Castle on the shores of Loch Ness — its spectacular ruins occupy a rocky promontory directly on the water and one of the Grant Tower's five storeys is accessible for a view over the loch. This castle was one of medieval Scotland's largest, and its history reads like a summary of the Wars of Independence: seized and re-seized between Scots and English in the 13th and 14th centuries, then attacked almost continuously by the Macdonald Lords of the Isles until 1545. Finally abandoned, its government troops blew up the gate in 1692 to prevent Jacobites using it. Those collapsed blocks you still see on the ground are from that explosion. Book your slot online: 547,000 people visited in 2019, Scotland's third most-visited site after Edinburgh and Stirling.

Eilean Donan Castle, halfway to Skye on the A87, is Scotland's most photographed castle — and it entirely justifies its reputation. Sitting on an islet at the confluence of three lochs, it was destroyed during the Jacobite wars of 1719 then entirely reconstructed between 1919 and 1932. Stop at least for photos from the bridge: the late-afternoon light on the golden walls with the lochs as backdrop is the image of Scotland.

Before crossing to Skye via the Kyle of Lochalsh bridge, stop at the Five Sisters of Kintail to look back towards the Highlands: five peaks exceeding 900 metres rise like a curtain behind Glen Shiel. It's one of the most striking mountain ranges on the Scottish mainland. You'll find a car park and viewpoint on the A87 — allow 20 minutes for photos.

Arrive in Portree in late afternoon — Skye's only real town, with its harbour ringed by colourful houses. Stroll along the quay to see the fishing boats and eat local seafood. Skye's lobster and crab rank among Scotland's finest.

The Old Man of Storr is Skye's most iconic image: a 55-metre rock column standing alone on a ridge above the Trotternish peninsula, north of Portree. The climb from the car park takes about 45 minutes — the trail is well-marked but heavily trafficked. Leave early morning to avoid tour buses. If mist shrouds the basalt columns, it's even more dramatic. From the top, the view across the Sound of Raasay and the islands below is vertiginous.

The Quiraing, further north on the same Trotternish peninsula, is landscape of another nature. Successive geological landslips have created a labyrinth of cliffs, pinnacles and high grassy ledges. The road skirting this slope from Uig is itself a spectacle — one of Skye's finest scenic drives. The hike into the Quiraing itself demands 2.5 to 3 hours for the full circuit.

The Fairy Glen near Uig is a small valley with spiralling grassy mounds that geology has sculpted into a limestone hollow — it's easy to see why locals associated these rounded forms with fairy rings. It's small, quiet and otherworldly. Allow 30 to 45 minutes for a circular walk around the mounds.

Kilt Rock on Trotternish's east coast merits a 20-minute stop: a basalt cliff of hexagonal columns plunging straight into the sea, with a waterfall leaping off the edge from the plateau. The resemblance to pleated kilt fabric is striking. There's a secure clifftop viewpoint — the views across the sea and jagged coastline are impressive.

The Fairy Pools of Glenbrittle are Skye's most popular hike — and they deserve their reputation. A series of translucent turquoise pools fed by waterfalls descends at the foot of the black Cuillin range. The water colour is real: reflections from sky and rocks create this blue-green even under clouds. The trail from the car park is easy, 2.4 km to the first pools. In 1601, these pools were the scene of a bloody battle between the Macdonalds of Sleat and the Macleods of Dunvegan — the waters supposedly ran red for days. Arrive as early as possible: tour buses arrive from 10am. Car park is paid but access to the pools is free.

Stop at the old Sligachan Bridge returning to Portree — this 19th-century stone bridge over the River Sligachan is one of the classic viewpoints of the Cuillin. Local legend says dipping your face in the river water for 7 seconds grants eternal beauty. The Sligachan Inn, open since 1830, faces the bridge and serves local whiskies.

activities.text: A cruise on Loch Ness from Clansman Harbour to Urquhart Castle is a different way to discover the site — you'll approach the ruins from the water, with the view of the Highlands unfolding behind. The cruise ticket includes castle entry. Operators run these cruises from Drumnadrochit. Book ahead in high season.

Horse riding across Skye's moorland from the Skye Riding Centre — a different experience to see the island from the saddle rather than on foot. The centre offers rides for all levels across open landscapes of the peninsula. Book in advance.

If you have energy and solid hiking boots, the walk to Talisker Bay on Skye's west coast rewards the effort: a black pebble beach at the foot of spectacular cliffs, with volcanic rocks sculpted by the sea and, on clear days, views to the Outer Hebrides. Allow 3 hours round trip from the Carbost car park. In the evening, stop at Talisker Distillery to sample a peaty whisky — it's Skye's only distillery.

Scotland Road Trip: Edinburgh, Highlands & Isle of Skye

Fairy Glen Uig Isle of Skye
Fairy Pools Glenbrittle Isle of Skye

visits.text: Five hundred years of Royal Navy concentrated in one place—that's Portsmouth Historic Dockyard. HMS Victory, Nelson's flagship at Trafalgar in 1805, is undergoing its biggest conservation project since construction, started in 2022. Still viewable: you board and watch shipwrights at work, an experience that'll vanish once the job finishes around 2032.

Sunk in 1545 in the Solent, the Mary Rose, Henry VIII's favourite ship, was raised in 1982 with 19,000 objects. Fragile timbers sprayed for 30 years to stabilize, displayed behind glass in a climate-controlled hall. World's largest Tudor artefact collection.

The Royal Navy's first iron-hulled warship, HMS Warrior 1860, never fired a shot in anger. Her mere existence rendered every wooden fleet obsolete. Visit the lower decks with costumed interpreters sometimes.

activities.text: Grab the Ultimate Explorer ticket (around £45) covering everything plus a harbour tour of about 45 minutes, valid one year. Allow a full day, don't rush it. Mary Rose Café or Camber Lights for breaks, with views of Royal Navy frigates on exercise.

days_plan.itin_title: England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton

visits.text: If you arrive at dawn in winter, thousands of wigeon cover the flooded meadows at Pulborough Brooks: a sound of continuous whistles impossible to describe otherwise. The RSPB reserve of 256 hectares in the Arun valley is designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest and Ramsar site. In summer, nightingales sing until June.

Pulborough Brooks, 256 hectares of wetland in the Arun Valley of the South Downs National Park: flood meadows, reedbeds, heaths and woods. The reserve hosts wintering duck and wader species, spring lapwings and nightingales in display, summer dragonflies. Views over the South Downs from the hides.

activities.text: Settle into one of the hides before 8 a.m. for the best observations. RSPB volunteers are on hand during the day to identify species. The Wetland Trail is closed to dogs—the woodland trail is open to them on leads.

days_plan.itin_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

visits.text: The Quiraing is Iceland in miniature without the flight or the expense. Landslips have sculpted a succession of rock tables, ridges, and corridors that clouds drift through in real time. The single-track road over Bealach Mòr pass is already a spectacle before you've laced your boots.

In foul weather, the Quiraing's rocks turn treacherous: the full circuit isn't recommended in driving rain. But even 200 m on the main path opens onto a striking panorama. The section to the first pinnacles is accessible in 20 minutes from the car park.

activities.text: Full 7 km circuit, 2 hours in good weather: you climb the ridges, descend into corridors, emerge cliff-side. The Table—a grassy plateau hidden behind a rock wall—is the reward of the long circuit. Discreet wild camping spots exist along the ridge.

days_plan.itin_title: Scotland: Highlands & Skye in 11 days

visits.text: London - Reform Club: Phileas Fogg bets he can travel around the world in eighty days, triggering a timed journey from the heart of London.
Port of London: embarkation for Suez, first maritime stage marking the passage from a regulated life to a race against time.
Return to London: Fogg believes he has lost his bet before realizing he has gained a day by crossing the globe eastward.
Reform Club: final scene where he presents himself at the last moment to prove he has accomplished the trip around the world within the deadline.

activities.text: Stroll between Westminster, the City and the clubs of Pall Mall to feel the Victorian atmosphere of Fogg's London.
Follow the Suez Canal and observe the ballet of ships, symbol of the shortening of distances in Fogg's era.
Retracing Fogg's steps through London by measuring the contrast between the fixity of the city and the movement of the journey accomplished.

days_plan.itin_title: Driving Phileas Fogg's Route: A Modern "Around the World in 80 Days" Itinerary
Driving Phileas Fogg's Route: A Modern "Around the World in 80 Days" Itinerary

visits.text: Regent's Park was designed by John Nash from 1811 commissioned by the Prince Regent (future George IV), who wanted a royal residence surrounded by gardens. The residence never materialized, but the 166 hectares of parkland remained. Queen Mary's Gardens contain one of England's largest rose gardens with over 12,000 roses of 85 varieties - at their peak in June. The Open Air Theatre, active since 1932, presents Shakespeare plays and musicals from April to September.

activities.text: Climb Primrose Hill, the hill north of the park that peaks at 78 metres - it's one of the best free panoramas over London's skyline, with the Shard, London Eye and City clearly identifiable. The spot is particularly popular with Londoners in the evening and on weekends.

London Zoo is on the park's northern edge since 1828 - it's the world's oldest scientific zoo still in operation. Allow half a day if you want to see it all. The Gorilla Kingdom, designed by architect Berthold Lubetkin in the 1930s, is listed for its modernist architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: What to See in London: Westminster, Museums & Tower Bridge

visits.text: Three Cliffs Bay is often cited as the most beautiful beach in Wales. Three limestone arches plunge straight into the sea at the end of a long sandy beach crossed by an estuary you must ford at low tide—which adds to the end-of-the-world feel. No car access to the beach: allow 25 minutes on foot from the Southgate or Pennard car park. The effort is amply rewarded.

On the ridge overlooking Three Cliffs Bay, the ruins of Pennard Castle emerge from the dunes. Built by the Normans in the 12th century to control the coastline, it has been gradually invaded by sand since the 14th century—local legend says it was buried as divine punishment. Access from the coastal path is immediate and the view from the ruined walls over the bay is striking.

On the way to Rhossili, Oxwich Bay deserves a stop: a wide 3 km beach facing south, sheltered by a pine forest and classified as a National Nature Reserve. The Oxwich Burrows dunes behind the beach are one of Gower's largest dune systems, home to rare plant species.

activities.text: The coastal loop from Southgate around Three Cliffs Bay and Pennard Burrows is one of the best walks in Gower: 8 km, little elevation, scenery that changes every ten minutes between cliffs, dunes, estuaries and beaches. Pass behind Pennard Castle for the plunging view over Three Cliffs from the rear.

days_plan.itin_title: South Wales Circuit: Mountains and Coast
Complete Wales: 12 Days North to South

visits.text: 24 arches, 400 metres, over 100 deaths according to the parish registers of Chapel-le-Dale: the human cost of Ribblehead Viaduct. Built between 1870 and 1875 for the Midland Railway, it stands 32 metres high and cost £343,000 at the time. The 2,300 navvies lived in camps christened Sebastopol and Belgravia. The Settle-Carlisle train still crosses.

activities.text: The wind whistles between the arches like inside an open-air cathedral. A 20-minute walk from the B6255 car park leads beneath the viaduct to appreciate the scale of the pillars. The Station Inn, right next door, offers pints with direct views - one of the finest outposts of its kind.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle

visits.text: Did you know that Richmond in Virginia, Richmond in Australia and Richmond-upon-Thames in London all owe their names to this small Yorkshire town? Henry Tudor had inherited the title Earl of Richmond; becoming Henry VII, he baptised his palace at Sheen with the name of his Yorkshire property - and the London district has borne that name ever since. Climb to Richmond Castle to understand why: perched on a cliff above the Swale, built by Alan the Red after the Norman Conquest around 1071, it possesses more 11th-century Norman architecture than any other castle in England. Local legend claims King Arthur and his knights sleep beneath the foundations - the English love their legends.

Don't miss the Georgian Theatre Royal just below the castle - it's Britain's most intact Georgian theatre, founded in 1788, closed in 1848 and used as a warehouse for a century before being restored and reopened in 1963. Its 200-seat auditorium with original painted decor is tiny and fascinating. If there's a performance during your visit, it's a rare experience not to be missed.

Take the road west from Richmond and you enter Swaledale - the wildest of the Yorkshire Dales. Dry-stone walls divide pastures to infinity, 18th-century hay barns punctuate the hills, villages like Reeth and Muker seem unchanged for two centuries. Drive to Keld at the valley's end for the Kisdon Force waterfalls - it's the road's terminus and everything beyond is on foot.

activities.text: Hiking in Swaledale to the hidden waterfalls around Keld: several short trails (2-4km) lead to Kisdon Force and Catrake Force from the village. The landscape of moorland and dry-stone walls is different from anything you've seen in the Lake District.

The Wensleydale Creamery at Hawes, heading up the valley of the same name from Richmond, offers creamery tours with tasting. Wensleydale is a crumbly, slightly honeyed cheese, produced in this valley since Cistercian monks from Roquefort settled here in 1156 and brought their maturing techniques. Hawes is about 45km from Richmond.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake District and North-East Loop: Lakes, Dales and Cathedrals
Yorkshire and North East England: Dales, Castles and Coastal Drama

visits.text: The Rye valley absorbs sound, moss covers everything, and Rievaulx's ruins emerge without warning. Northern England's first Cistercian abbey, founded in 1132, housed up to 140 monks and 500 lay brothers under Abbot Aelred. Dissolved in 1538, 22 monks still remained. From Rievaulx Terrace above, the view of ruins in their wooded bowl ranks among England's most arresting. Less crowded than Fountains; lunch in Helmsley afterwards.

days_plan.itin_title: Gothic Yorkshire: Dracula, Abbeys & Witches – 8 days

visits.text: Smugglers' village: too beautiful to be honest. Robin Hood's Bay, clinging to the cliff, served as the Yorkshire coast's principal smuggling port in the 18th century. Local legend claims a bale of French silk could traverse the entire village from beach to summit without ever seeing daylight, passing cellar to cellar. Lanes still bear names like Brandy Alley. Look for wall hooks and concealed passages.

activities.text: Legend says a bale of French silk crossed the entire village without seeing daylight – beach to summit – via a network of tunnels connecting cellars. Local historians have uncovered bricked-up passages and unusual cellars, without confirming the network's full extent. The Robin Hood's Bay and Fylingdales Museum, volunteer-run, preserves smuggling archives. Walk down to the beach, exposed only at low tide.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern England: 12 Days from Manchester to Newcastle
Gothic Yorkshire: Dracula, Abbeys & Witches – 8 days

visits.text: The Royal Academy of Arts has occupied Burlington House on Piccadilly since 1868. Founded in 1768 with Joshua Reynolds as first president, it's the UK's oldest institution dedicated to the fine arts. Its temporary exhibitions are among London's most sought-after—the annual Summer Exhibition, running since 1769 without interruption, is the world's longest-running exhibition series. Auguste Rodin exhibited here in 1914 during his first major exhibition in his lifetime, before bequeathing his sculptures to the institution as a symbol of Franco-British friendship. Check the programme before visiting: the permanent collections are less spectacular than the major temporary exhibitions.

activities.text: Check the current exhibition programme on the Royal Academy website before planning your visit—some blockbuster exhibitions sell out weeks in advance.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: On the way from Brighton, detour to Hever Castle: Anne Boleyn's childhood castle, Henry VIII's second wife, lies about twenty minutes north of Tunbridge Wells. Built in the late 13th century and surrounded by a double moat, it houses original Tudor portraits and the two prayer books annotated in Anne's hand. The gardens created in the early 20th century by American millionaire William Waldorf Astor span 60 hectares and offer one of Kent's most beautiful settings.

Tunbridge Wells developed around a chalybeate spring discovered in 1606, which attracted all of English aristocracy for two centuries. The historic heart is the Pantiles—a 17th-century tree-lined colonnade bordered by shops. Its name comes from the pan tiles that originally paved the ground. Princess Anne donated £100 in 1698 to repair the paving after her son slipped. Today, the Pantiles house restaurants, markets, and open-air concerts. Look for the chalybeate spring still in operation—you can drink the iron-rich water according to a 400-year tradition.

activities.text: About twenty minutes southeast of Tunbridge Wells, Scotney Castle offers one of Kent's most romantic tableaux: the ruins of a 14th-century medieval castle in the middle of a lake, surrounded by English-style gardens planted with rhododendrons. In May-June, the blooming is spectacular. Managed by the National Trust.

Kent is England's garden—literally. Several vineyards in the region offer tastings: the Medway Valley and hills around Tunbridge Wells produce sparkling wines comparable to champagne. Plan to contact an estate in advance to arrange a visit and tasting.

days_plan.itin_title: Southeast England & Kent Circuit: White Cliffs and Secret Gardens
Surrey to Kent: Castles and Gardens in 10 Days

visits.text: Rye is arguably the best-preserved medieval town in southeast England. Perched on a hill that was once an island surrounded by marshes, it was part of the Cinque Ports—the confederation of ports that supplied ships to the Crown in exchange for trading privileges. Start with Mermaid Street: this isn't a reconstruction, it's one of England's oldest streets, with timber-framed houses dating back to the 12th century. The Mermaid Inn was rebuilt in 1420 after a French raid; in the 18th century, the Hawkhurst Gang, a terrifying band of smugglers, made it their headquarters. Legend has it that one evening, twenty gang members fired shots in the neighboring street for fun, and a too-curious passerby, James Marshall, disappeared without a trace.

Ypres Tower, built in 1249 to defend the town from French attacks, is Rye's oldest monument. It served successively as a fortress, private residence, courthouse, prison, and morgue before becoming a museum. Climb to the top for views over the Rother estuary and Romney Marsh in the distance. Don't miss the prison cells and the medieval herb garden below. The Women's Tower in the same complex was, in 1857, the country's first women-only prison.

Lamb House, on Church Square, is one of Kent's most intimate literary addresses. Henry James lived here from 1898 to 1916 and wrote several of his major novels here, including The Wings of the Dove. E.F. Benson succeeded him and transformed Rye into Tilling, the setting for his comic series Mapp and Lucia. The Georgian house and its walled garden now belong to the National Trust. Allow 45 minutes for the visit.

Finish your tour at St Mary's Church, whose history goes back 900 years. The chiming clock installed in 1561 is the oldest church clock with original mechanism still operating in the UK. Climb the bell tower—entry is paid but modest—for panoramic views over Rye's rooftops, the marshes, and, on clear days, out to sea.

activities.text: Get lost in Rye's twittens—those narrow Sussex alleyways that connect streets. They were much favored by smugglers in the 18th century for slipping between houses unseen. Rye is also known for its antique dealers; if you enjoy browsing, the Strand and its surroundings have several shops worth checking out. If you'd like to pick up a good bottle for the next leg, Gusbourne and Oxney Organic Estate vineyards are about 20 minutes' drive away.

In the evening, settle in at the Ypres Castle Inn, a 17th-century pub nestled in the tower gardens, with a lovely walled garden in summer and a stone fireplace in winter. The place is frequented by locals—you won't find the touristy pub atmosphere of the town center.

days_plan.itin_title: Southeast England & Kent Circuit: White Cliffs and Secret Gardens
Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports

visits.text: The dark timbers of Saffron Walden's Tudor houses frame streets that nobody ever had to rebuild—the decline of the wool trade in the sixteenth century preserved the town better than any conservation scheme. Church Street and Bridge Street are the most striking examples. The Saturday morning market has animated the square for centuries.

'Never rebuilt, never sacked': Audley End House is one of England's finest Jacobean examples. Henry VIII gave the estate to Sir Thomas Audley after the dissolution of the monasteries, and successive owners preserved its state rooms, Victorian kitchens, and gardens designed by Capability Brown. Walking distance from the station, 2km.

activities.text: You wander through the Bridge End Gardens, a Victorian plant maze hidden behind an unassuming gate. These garden rooms—formal lawns, rose garden, kitchen garden—were created by local banker Gibson in the nineteenth century. Free entry; the hedge maze is the only element requiring a key available from the tourist office.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia, Cambridge & Rutland: 13-Day Road Trip

visits.text: The needle-sharp spire soars 123 metres into the Wiltshire sky—it's been Britain's tallest church spire since 1561. The cathedral was built in just 38 years (1220-1258), giving it a rare stylistic unity among England's great Gothic cathedrals. In the Chapter House, one of the four original Magna Carta manuscripts from 1215 is on display: the best preserved of the four surviving copies. The nave clock, dating from around 1386, is considered the world's oldest working mechanical clock. Avoid Sunday mornings when access is restricted to services.

Ten minutes' walk from the centre, the Town Path runs along Avon water meadows and offers the postcard view of the spire—yet it's rarely crowded. The Cathedral Close, England's largest, is ringed by inhabited medieval and Georgian houses. The National Trust's Mompesson House opens its eighteenth-century drawing rooms to visitors. Best in late afternoon when the low-angled light turns the stone golden.

activities.text: Stroll through the Cathedral Close in the evening after the tour buses have departed. The Old Mill pub at the end of Town Path serves local Badger Ale with views over the river.

days_plan.itin_title: England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton
Deep Wiltshire: from Salisbury to Newbury in 7 days

places.name: Saltburn By The Sea

visits.text: Sandsend, 5 kilometres north of Whitby, is a sand-and-shingle beach on the Jurassic coast—one of the richest on the English coast for marine fossils. Ammonites, belemnites and marine reptile teeth from the Upper Jurassic regularly emerge from the cliff after storms. No special equipment needed: the shoreline at low tide is enough. The beach is uncrowded outside summer.

Staithes, wedged between two headlands (Cowbar Nab and Penny Nab) at the mouth of a ravine, is one of the most authentic fishing villages on the north coast. Its lanes are so narrow—the narrowest, Dog Loup, is 45 cm wide—that the sun only enters at midday. Leave your car at the clifftop car park and walk down. In 1745, the young James Cook, aged 16, came here to work as a grocer's apprentice under William Sanderson. He didn't last 18 months: fascinated by the boats in the harbour, he left for Whitby to join the merchant navy. Sanderson's shop was destroyed by a storm but its materials were reused to build a cottage you'll still find on Church Street.

The Captain Cook and Staithes Heritage Centre, in the former 1880 Methodist chapel at the top of the village, holds over 200 books, 61 original engravings from Cook's voyages and dozens of objects related to his life. The upper floor recreates William Sanderson's shop as it was in 1745—the only such recreation in the world. Free entry, donations welcome.

The Saltburn Cliff Tramway is the oldest water-balanced cliff lift still operating in the UK. Opened in June 1884, it connects the Victorian town atop the cliff to the pier and beach 37 metres below, on 63 metres of track at a 71% gradient. Its operating principle has barely changed in 140 years: a 1,100-litre water tank is filled in the upper car until its weight exceeds that of the lower car—the journey from one level to the other takes 55 seconds, controlled by a brakesman in person. Allow 15 minutes for the round trip.

Saltburn's Victorian pier, opened in May 1869 and Grade II* listed, is the only major pleasure pier surviving on the entire North-East coast of England. It extends 200 metres over the North Sea. Walk to the end to see the coast recede in both directions—on clear days, the Boulby cliffs to the north (England's highest, at 203 metres) are visible.

Saltburn-by-the-Sea beach, a long strip of sand and shingle at the foot of the cliffs, is a recognised surf spot on the north coast. Beyond the water, the Victorian seafront—colourful bathing huts, esplanades, valley gardens in the background—is intact and particularly photogenic at sunset when the light takes on an orange tint.

activities.text: Fossil hunting on Sandsend beach and cliffs: armed with a sharp eye and patience, you'll find ammonites on the surface among the pebbles after storms. Operators like Yorkshire Coast Rock run guided outings on this coastal stretch if you want to stack the odds—book ahead, groups are small.

Walk down into Staithes to the harbour: allow 15 minutes downhill (very steep for the last 200 metres) and 20 minutes back up. At the bottom, the quay is tiny, the cobles (small flat-bottomed boats) are often hauled onto the bank. Take time to follow Staithes Beck to the sea. Don't attempt to drive down—the lanes are too narrow for any non-resident vehicle.

Surfing or stand-up paddleboarding lesson on Saltburn beach: hire operators work on the main beach in season. The Saltburn surf break is one of the most reputed in northern England—consistent swell, sandy bottom, cold water but viable in a wetsuit. If you'd rather watch, stay atop the cliff and watch the surfers battle the tide.

Ride the Saltburn Cliff Tramway: take the car both ways if you want to see the mechanism at work, and watch the brakesman operate at the top. The view over the beach and pier from the tracks, mid-slope, is one of the finest of the leg.

days_plan.itin_title: Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands
Yorkshire: Wild Coast & Secret Moorlands
North York Moors Coast Road Trip: Cliffs and Fishing Villages

map_keywords: Captain Cook and Staithes Heritage Centre

visits.text: Sandringham Estate has been the British royal family's private residence since 1862, when Queen Victoria purchased it for her eldest son, the future Edward VII, for £220,000. The current red-brick Jacobean-style house was built in 1870 by architect A.J. Humbert. George V wrote of Sandringham: 'the place I love better than anywhere in the world'. George VI died here in 1952. Eight ground-floor rooms, virtually unchanged since Edwardian times, open to the public from late March to early October. The 24-hectare Country Park is open year-round for free.

St Mary Magdalene church, on the Sandringham estate, is the royal family's parish church—where the Christmas service watched by Britons on television each year is held. Built in the 14th century and extensively remodelled by Edward VII, it houses several royal memorials. Don't miss the Silver Jubilee Gate in solid silver, a gift from the royal family in 1977.

activities.text: After the house and church, wander freely in Sandringham Country Park—24 hectares of woods and heathland open year-round. In season, the estate's organic farm sells its own produce at the shop. It's a good moment to catch your breath before the final day.

days_plan.itin_title: East Anglia Circuit: Cambridge, Suffolk Coast and Royal Norfolk

visits.text: The RSPB reserve at Bempton Cliffs, about 30 kilometres south of Scarborough, is one of England's great wildlife experiences. The chalk cliffs rise 120 metres above the North Sea, and between April and July they host over 200,000 nesting seabirds—the only mainland gannet colony in all of Great Britain (more than 11,000 pairs), plus puffins, guillemots, razorbills and kittiwakes. Gannets have a 2-metre wingspan and sometimes glide eye-level past the viewing platforms. Bring binoculars—puffins blend in among the guillemots. Best period: May to July.

Flamborough Head, the chalk promontory jutting into the North Sea, has two lighthouses: the old one (1674, in white chalk, England's oldest intact lighthouse) and the new one (1806, still in service). From the coastal paths around the headland, you look straight down sheer cliffs to sea caves below—keep an eye out for seals sometimes basking on the rocks.

Scarborough Castle dominates a 91-metre-high rocky headland with cliffs on three sides, between the town's two bays. The site has been occupied for 3,000 years: Roman signal station in the 4th century, Anglo-Saxon chapel, then royal fortress from 1159 when Henry II seized it and built the great stone keep. It was here that Edward II's favourite, Piers Gaveston, took refuge in 1312 before being captured. In December 1914, German naval bombardment (SMS Derfflinger and SMS Von der Tann) damaged the keep—the shell impacts are still visible. Managed by English Heritage, it's a vast 16-acre site with panoramic views over both bays. Allow 1h30.

South Bay, the great sandy beach sheltered below the castle, is Scarborough's historic beach—the town claims to be England's first seaside resort, having attracted spa visitors to its mineral springs as early as the 1620s. In season, the colourful bathing huts and the Victorian Spa are an almost intact period backdrop. For you, the essentials: a seaside walk and an ice cream.

The Scarborough Maritime Heritage Centre, near the old harbour, traces the town's history of deep-sea fishing and maritime trade. Collections of model boats, fishing gear and fishermen's family testimonies. Small but well documented, the place is often quiet—an hour suffices.

activities.text: The coastal path around the castle headland links South Bay to North Bay via the ramparts—a 45-minute walk with plunging views over both bays and the fishing harbour below. Start from the castle entrance and descend to the old harbour on the far side to see the fishing boats.

North Bay, quieter and less touristy than South Bay, is Scarborough's surf spot. If you don't have a board, local operators offer lessons and rentals on the beach in season. The water's cold even in summer—wetsuit essential.

days_plan.itin_title: North York Moors Coast Road Trip: Cliffs and Fishing Villages

visits.text: The Science Museum in South Kensington holds over 300,000 objects in its collections and houses one of the most striking pieces in all of London: the Apollo 10 capsule nicknamed Charlie Brown, the only Apollo spacecraft displayed outside the United States. In May 1969, Thomas Stafford, John Young and Eugene Cernan conducted a dress rehearsal for the first Moon landing in this capsule, approaching within 14.5 km of the lunar surface without touching down. The capsule arrived at the Science Museum in 1976 and has resided in the new Space gallery inaugurated in 2025, alongside the Soyuz TMA-19M capsule that brought British astronaut Tim Peake back to Earth. You can still see the hand-scribbled calculations by astronauts on the interior walls. Nearby in the Making the Modern World gallery, don't miss Stephenson's Rocket from 1829 and the first commercial steam engine.

activities.text: If you're visiting with children under 14, the Wonderlab is an interactive space dedicated to scientific experiments—plan for a separate ticket and book ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: London's Major Museums and Galleries

visits.text: The Seven Sisters draw seven white chalk humps plunging sheer into the Channel. The loop from Cuckmere Haven to the first cliffs covers about 8 km, ideal for a half-day. No barriers, unstable cliffs—stay back from the edge.

Backdrop for numerous films (including Atonement), Cuckmere Haven sees its river meander in oxbows to the sea. The coastguard cottages perched above the beach face erosion threats, several collapses in recent years.

activities.text: Park at Seven Sisters Country Park (Exceat) or South Hill Barn in Seaford. The easy-access trail departs from South Hill Barn and stays accessible to pushchairs and wheelchairs, with identical views of the cliffs. The Cuckmere Inn for an end-of-walk pint.

days_plan.itin_title: England's South Coast 6d: Bristol to Brighton

visits.text: Film set and real village: Shere plays both roles effortlessly. The Holiday was shot here in 2006 with artificial snow—the village is already so photogenic that producers dared not alter it. The Tillingbourne flows under a low stone bridge through the main street, ducks linger there, and the 16th-17th century half-timbered houses lean slightly toward it. Fewer than 1,000 residents, no traffic lights, a handful of shops.

Mentioned in the Domesday Book, Shere's St James's Church dates in its current form from the 12th century. Inside is a tiny cell walled up in which Christine Carpenter, a religious anchoress, voluntarily enclosed herself in the 14th century. The window through which food was passed to her is still visible. Free entry, generally open during the day.

activities.text: Walk from the village toward Newlands Corner, a viewpoint about 3 km into the Surrey Hills where on clear days the vista extends to the South Downs. It's near here that Agatha Christie's car was found in 1926 when the novelist staged her own disappearance. Well-marked path, roughly 90 minutes return.

days_plan.itin_title: Surrey to Kent: Castles and Gardens in 10 Days

places.name: Shiplake
